GK-ALY-001.weeklyFact: Sweet alyssum's honey-like scent is strong enough to be noticeable from several feet away on a warm day, despite the tiny individual flowers.
weeklyFacts: Sweet alyssum's honey-like scent is strong enough to be noticeable from several feet away on a warm day, despite the tiny individual flowers.
Alyssum is frequently sown deliberately alongside vegetable crops specifically to draw in hoverflies, whose larvae are voracious aphid predators.
GK-ALY-001.pollinatorFact: Alyssum punches well above its size for pollinators — its honey-scented flowers are a strong draw for hoverflies and small bees, which is why it's often sown deliberately near vegetable crops.

GK-SNAP-001.name: Snapdragon
GK-SNAP-001.subtitle: Tall spires of jaw-shaped flowers, a cut-flower classic that self-seeds in mild gardens.

quickCare.Water: Water regularly while establishing; reasonably drought-tolerant once settled
quickCare.Light: Full sun
quickCare.Feeding: Light feed at planting; potash feed once flowering for cut varieties
quickCare.Pruning: Pinch young plants to encourage branching; deadhead spikes as they fade

GK-SNAP-001.notes: Squeeze a snapdragon flower gently from the sides — the 'jaws' opening and closing is where the name comes from, and it's a nice thing to show curious children.

seasonalActions.Spring: Sow indoors in late winter for the strongest cut-flower stems, or plant out young plants after frost.
seasonalActions.Summer: Pinch young plants to encourage branching; deadhead as spikes fade.
seasonalActions.Autumn: Often reflowers from a second flush if cut back after the first.
seasonalActions.Winter: Sometimes survives mild winters as a short-lived perennial, though usually grown as an annual.

problems.name: Rust (orange pustules on leaves)
problems.fix: Remove affected foliage promptly, improve airflow and avoid overhead watering — can spread quickly in humid conditions.

problems.name: Powdery mildew late season
problems.fix: Water at the base and space plants for good airflow.

propagation.season: Sow indoors in late winter — seed is fine and needs light to germinate, so don't bury it.
propagation.methods: Named varieties are usually grown from seed rather than cuttings.
propagation.tips: A slower starter than many annuals, but reliable once underway.
propagation.tracker: Propagation tracker coming soon — for now, add dates, method and result in your notes.

pollinator.value: Snapdragon's closed 'jaw' flowers need a bee heavy enough to pry them open, which is exactly the mechanism that gives the plant its name.

timeline.Pruning: Pinch when young; deadhead spent spikes

Dried snapdragon seed pods split open into a shape that eerily resembles a tiny skull, which has made them a minor internet curiosity.
GK-SNAP-001.pollinatorFact: Snapdragon flowers are specifically adapted to require a heavy enough bee, usually a bumblebee, to force the hinged petals open and reach the nectar inside.

GK-VBN-001.name: Verbena
GK-VBN-001.subtitle: Airy, see-through perennial (often grown as an annual) that towers over a border on wiry stems.

quickCare.Water: Drought-tolerant once established; water while young
quickCare.Light: Full sun
quickCare.Feeding: Little to no feeding needed
quickCare.Pruning: Cut back in spring; deadhead through summer if tidiness matters

GK-VBN-001.soil: Free-draining soil; tolerates poor, dry conditions well.
GK-VBN-001.medium: Peat-free compost with added grit for containers.

seasonalActions.Spring: Cut back old growth to fresh shoots at the base.
seasonalActions.Summer: Flowers continuously; deadhead if wanted, though not essential.
seasonalActions.Autumn: Often self-seeds; leave some seed heads for birds and volunteers.
seasonalActions.Winter: May survive as a short-lived perennial in mild, well-drained gardens; often grown as an annual elsewhere.

problems.name: Winter losses in wet, heavy soil
problems.fix: Poor drainage is the main killer, not cold; improve drainage or treat as an annual in heavy clay gardens.

problems.name: Powdery mildew late season
problems.fix: Improve airflow; rarely serious enough to threaten the plant.

propagation.season: Self-seeds readily — often the easiest 'propagation' method is simply to let it seed around.
propagation.methods: Softwood cuttings in late spring root reasonably well for guaranteed reliable stock.
propagation.tips: Sow fresh seed in warmth for a more controlled start.
propagation.tracker: Propagation tracker coming soon — for now, add dates, method and result in your notes.

pollinator.value: One of the standout butterfly and bee plants in this library — tall verbena is a genuine magnet for a huge range of pollinators over a very long season.

weeklyFacts: Verbena bonariensis is native to South America but has naturalised so successfully in some mild UK and Irish gardens that it self-seeds almost like a native wildflower.

GK-CLEO-001.name: Cleome
GK-CLEO-001.subtitle: Tall, dramatic, slightly spiky annual with striking spidery flowers.

quickCare.Water: Water regularly, especially in dry spells — a thirsty plant for its size
quickCare.Light: Full sun

quickCare.Pruning: Deadhead spent flower spikes to prolong flowering; wear gloves, as stems can be slightly sticky and spiny

seasonalActions.Spring: Sow indoors in warmth, or direct after frost risk passes.
seasonalActions.Summer: Water regularly and deadhead to keep flowering going.
seasonalActions.Autumn: Continues until the first frost; collect seed pods if wanted.
seasonalActions.Winter: N/A — annual, finished for the year.

problems.name: Skin irritation from handling
problems.fix: Wear gloves when deadheading or cutting — the stems are mildly spiny and sticky.

problems.name: Aphids on flower buds
problems.fix: Wash off with water or encourage ladybirds.

GK-AGE-001.name: Ageratum
GK-AGE-001.subtitle: Compact, fluffy-flowered edging annual in an unusually true powder blue.

quickCare.Light: Full sun to part shade
quickCare.Feeding: Light feed through summer for continuous flowering
quickCare.Pruning: Deadhead spent flower clusters to prolong the display

GK-AGE-001.notes: One of relatively few common bedding annuals in a genuinely true blue, rather than purple-blue — useful for colour-scheme planning.

seasonalActions.Spring: Plant out after frost risk has passed.
seasonalActions.Summer: Water consistently and deadhead through summer.
seasonalActions.Autumn: Continues flowering until the first frost.
seasonalActions.Winter: N/A — annual, finished for the year.

problems.name: Powdery mildew in humid weather
problems.fix: Improve airflow between plants and avoid overhead watering.

problems.name: Leggy growth in too much shade
problems.fix: Move to a sunnier spot for compact, floriferous growth.

propagation.season: Usually grown from seed sown indoors in warmth in early spring.
propagation.methods: Seed needs light to germinate — sow on the surface, don't bury.
propagation.tips: Cuttings are possible but seed is the more common, reliable method.
propagation.tracker: Propagation tracker coming soon — for now, add dates, method and result in your notes.

weeklyFacts: Ageratum's fluffy flower texture comes from unusually long, thread-like petals rather than the flat petals most flowers have.
The genus name Ageratum comes from Greek for 'un-aging,' referring to how long the flower colour holds without fading.

GK-STO-001.name: Stock
GK-STO-001.subtitle: Old-fashioned, intensely fragrant cottage garden annual, especially scented at dusk.

quickCare.Water: Water regularly while establishing; avoid letting it dry out during flowering
quickCare.Light: Full sun to light shade
quickCare.Feeding: Light feed at planting
quickCare.Pruning: Deadhead spent flower spikes to prolong the display

GK-STO-001.notes: Plant near a path, door or seating area — the fragrance is genuinely one of the best reasons to grow this rather than a purely visual one.

seasonalActions.Spring: Sow indoors in early spring, or direct once soil has warmed.
seasonalActions.Summer: Deadhead spikes as they fade to prolong flowering.
seasonalActions.Autumn: May continue flowering into autumn in mild weather.
seasonalActions.Winter: N/A — annual, finished for the year, though some varieties can be sown for a spring display too.

problems.name: Clubroot in acidic, poorly drained soil
problems.fix: Improve drainage and avoid very acidic soil; rotate planting areas if this recurs.

problems.name: Aphids on flower spikes
problems.fix: Wash off with water or encourage ladybirds.

propagation.season: Sow indoors in early spring for a summer display, or in late summer for overwintering and spring flowering in mild areas.
propagation.methods: Seed needs light to germinate — sow on the surface.
propagation.tips: Named double-flowered forms are usually grown from seed selected to produce a high proportion of doubles.
propagation.tracker: Propagation tracker coming soon — for now, add dates, method and result in your notes.

pollinator.value: Strongly evening-scented, and popular with moths at dusk as well as bees and butterflies during the day.

GK-STO-001.weeklyFact: Stock's fragrance intensifies noticeably in the evening, an adaptation to attract night-flying moths when few visual cues are left to guide them.

GK-CANT-001.name: Cornflower
GK-CANT-001.subtitle: Wildflower-meadow annual in vivid true blue, beloved by bees.

quickCare.Water: Water while establishing; drought-tolerant once settled
quickCare.Light: Full sun
quickCare.Feeding: Little to no feeding needed — thrives in poor soil
quickCare.Pruning: Deadhead regularly to prolong flowering and control self-seeding

GK-CANT-001.notes: A genuine former wildflower, once so common in British and Irish cornfields it inspired its name — now largely confined to gardens due to modern farming.
GK-CANT-001.soil: Poor to average, free-draining soil — avoid rich, fertile ground.
GK-CANT-001.medium: Peat-free compost; not fussy, thrives on relative neglect.

seasonalActions.Spring: Sow direct in poor soil for the best display.
seasonalActions.Summer: Deadhead regularly; continues flowering strongly.
seasonalActions.Autumn: Self-seeds readily — leave some seed heads for birds and volunteers.
seasonalActions.Winter: Autumn-sown plants may overwinter as small rosettes in mild areas for earlier summer flowering.

problems.name: Powdery mildew late season
problems.fix: Improve airflow; rarely serious enough to threaten the display.

problems.name: Flopping in rich soil or too much shade
problems.fix: Move future sowings to poorer soil in full sun for sturdier growth.

propagation.season: Sow direct — genuinely one of the easiest wildflower-style annuals to establish from seed.
propagation.methods: Self-seeds so readily that a single good year can supply seed for several more.
propagation.tips: Autumn sowing in mild areas often gives stronger, earlier-flowering plants than spring sowing.

weeklyFacts: Cornflower was once so common in European grain fields that it's named directly for growing among corn (cereal crops) — modern herbicides have made truly wild cornflower rare.

GK-CANT-001.pollinatorFact: Cornflower is an outstanding bee plant, and its open flower structure is specifically valuable to a range of solitary bee species alongside honeybees and bumblebees.

GK-POP-001.name: Poppy
GK-POP-001.subtitle: Delicate, papery-petalled wildflower annual, quick and easy from seed.

quickCare.Water: Water while establishing; drought-tolerant once settled
quickCare.Light: Full sun
quickCare.Feeding: No feeding needed — thrives in poor soil
quickCare.Pruning: No deadheading required, though it prolongs flowering slightly; leave some seed heads to self-seed

GK-POP-001.notes: Each individual poppy flower lasts only a day or two, but a well-established patch keeps producing new ones continuously through summer.
GK-POP-001.soil: Poor, free-draining soil — famously thrives in disturbed, low-fertility ground.
GK-POP-001.medium: Peat-free compost is unnecessary richness for this plant; garden soil is usually fine.

seasonalActions.Spring: Sow direct — seed needs light and disturbed soil to germinate well, mimicking its natural habitat.
seasonalActions.Summer: Flowers continuously through summer with minimal input.
seasonalActions.Autumn: Leave seed heads to scatter for next year's display.
seasonalActions.Winter: Self-sown seedlings may appear as small rosettes over winter.

problems.name: Poor germination
problems.fix: Usually seed sown too deep or into undisturbed soil; rake the surface and sow on top, barely covering.

problems.name: Flopping after rain
problems.fix: Normal for the delicate petals; new flowers open continuously to replace them.

propagation.season: Sow direct on raked, disturbed soil — genuinely one of the simplest annuals to grow from seed.
propagation.methods: Self-seeds prolifically once established; often needs no resowing after the first year.
propagation.tips: Seed remains viable in soil for years, which is why poppies famously reappear after ground is disturbed.
propagation.tracker: Propagation tracker coming soon — for now, add dates, method and result in your notes.

pollinator.value: Poppy pollen and open flower structure make it a strong draw for bees, and its long historical association with disturbed farmland made it one of the most recognisable bee-friendly wildflowers in Europe.

timeline.Pruning: No pruning needed; leave seed heads to scatter

GK-POP-001.weeklyFact: Field poppy seeds can remain dormant but viable in undisturbed soil for decades, which is exactly why poppies famously bloomed across churned World War One battlefields.
weeklyFacts: Field poppy seeds can remain dormant but viable in undisturbed soil for decades, which is exactly why poppies famously bloomed across churned World War One battlefields.
The poppy's seed pod releases seed through small pores at the top, shaking out like a pepper pot when the stem sways in the wind.
GK-POP-001.pollinatorFact: Poppy's open flower structure and abundant pollen make it a genuinely strong draw for bees, and its long association with disturbed farmland ground made it one of Europe's most recognisable pollinator wildflowers.

GK-PAN-001.name: Pansy

weeklyFacts: Pansy flowers have a distinctive 'face' marking that comes from patterns of ultraviolet-reflecting cells invisible to humans but clearly visible to bees, guiding them to the nectar.

GK-PIE-001.name: Pieris 'Little Heath'

quickCare.Pruning: Trim lightly after flowering to shape; avoid hard pruning into old wood

seasonalActions.Spring: New growth flushes bright pink-red — protect from late frost, which can damage this tender new foliage.

propagation.season: Semi-ripe cuttings in summer root reasonably well in a gritty, ericaceous mix.
propagation.methods: Layering a low branch is a slower but reliable alternative.
propagation.tips: Being a named cultivar, cuttings (not seed) are the way to keep the same compact, variegated form.
propagation.tracker: Propagation tracker coming soon — for now, add dates, method and result in your notes.

pollinator.value: Small bell-shaped spring flowers, similar in shape to its heather-family relatives, are visited by early-season bees.

timeline.Pruning: Trim lightly after flowering, avoiding old wood

photoPlan.hero: Clear whole-plant photo of Pieris 'Little Heath'.

GK-PIE-001.weeklyFact: Pieris is in the same plant family as rhododendrons and blueberries — all lovers of acidic soil.
weeklyFacts: Pieris is in the same plant family as rhododendrons and blueberries — all lovers of acidic soil.
'Little Heath' was specifically bred and selected for its dwarf habit, making it useful in smaller gardens and containers where the full-sized species would be too large.
GK-PIE-001.pollinatorFact: Pieris's small bell-shaped spring flowers are similar in structure to its heather-family relatives and provide a useful early-season nectar source for bees.

GK-TRA-001.name: Tradescantia 'Purple Heart'
GK-TRA-001.subtitle: Trailing houseplant with vivid deep-purple foliage, easy and fast-growing.

quickCare.Water: Water when the top of the compost is dry; more drought-tolerant than most trailing houseplants
quickCare.Light: Bright light, some direct sun for the deepest purple colour — too little light fades the colour towards green
quickCare.Feeding: Light feed in the growing season
quickCare.Pruning: Trim leggy stems to encourage bushier growth; pinch tips regularly

GK-TRA-001.notes: Colour intensity is genuinely light-dependent — a plant kept in low light will noticeably green up compared to one in bright, sunny conditions.
GK-TRA-001.soil: N/A — grown in potting compost, or outdoors in mild climates as ground cover.
GK-TRA-001.medium: Peat-free houseplant compost with reasonable drainage.

seasonalActions.Spring: Resume feeding as growth picks up.
seasonalActions.Summer: Water more often; trim leggy stems to keep it compact.
seasonalActions.Autumn: Reduce feeding as growth slows.
seasonalActions.Winter: Water sparingly; can survive as a houseplant year-round with reduced care.

problems.name: Leggy, sparse growth
problems.fix: Trim back hard — it regrows quickly and bushier from a firm pinch or cut.

problems.name: Fading to green in low light
problems.fix: Move to a brighter spot with some direct sun to restore the deep purple colour.

propagation.season: Stem cuttings root extremely easily in water within about a week — one of the fastest, most reliable houseplant propagations going.
propagation.methods: Simply place a cut piece with a node into water or moist compost.
propagation.tips: A genuinely great 'give a cutting to a friend' plant, since it roots so readily.
propagation.tracker: Propagation tracker coming soon — for now, add dates, method and result in your notes.

pollinator.value: Small three-petaled pink-purple flowers occasionally appear and can attract pollinators where grown outdoors in warm climates.

timeline.Flowering: Occasional small flowers, mainly in bright conditions
timeline.Pruning: Trim and pinch leggy stems as needed

photoPlan.hero: Clear whole-plant photo of Tradescantia 'Purple Heart'.
gallery: Whole trailing plant showing purple colour

GK-TRA-001.weeklyFact: Tradescantia pallida's deep purple pigment is the same class of compound (anthocyanin) responsible for purple cabbage and red autumn leaves — it's a genuine sun-protection pigment, not just decoration.
weeklyFacts: Tradescantia pallida's deep purple pigment is the same class of compound (anthocyanin) responsible for purple cabbage and red autumn leaves — it's a genuine sun-protection pigment, not just decoration.
'Purple Heart' roots so readily from cuttings that some gardeners use it as a fast, disposable trailing filler for summer containers, replacing it each year rather than overwintering it.
GK-TRA-001.pollinatorFact: Tradescantia's small flowers occasionally attract pollinators when grown outdoors in warm climates, though indoors it's grown almost entirely for its striking purple foliage.

GK-DAF-001.name: Daffodil
GK-DAF-001.subtitle: The classic reliable spring bulb — plant once, enjoy for decades.

quickCare.Water: Water at planting, then rely on rainfall — avoid summer watering while dormant
quickCare.Light: Full sun to light shade
quickCare.Feeding: Light high-potash feed as leaves emerge, if flowering declines
quickCare.Pruning: Deadhead after flowering; never cut or tie foliage down early

GK-DAF-001.notes: Deer- and squirrel-resistant thanks to a natural toxin, which is part of why daffodils are such a reliable, low-drama bulb choice.
GK-DAF-001.medium: Peat-free compost with added grit for containers.

seasonalActions.Spring: Flowers open; deadhead as they fade, but leave foliage standing.
seasonalActions.Summer: Foliage dies back naturally — this is when the bulb recharges for next year.
seasonalActions.Autumn: Plant new bulbs from September, deep enough to avoid frost heave.
seasonalActions.Winter: Bulbs are dormant underground; no care needed.

problems.name: Narcissus bulb fly
problems.fix: Larvae hollow out bulbs; lift and discard any soft, hollow bulbs found when dividing clumps.

problems.name: Poor flowering after a few years ('blindness')
problems.fix: Usually overcrowding or foliage cut too early; divide congested clumps and always let leaves die back naturally.

propagation.season: Bulbs naturally multiply and can be lifted and divided every few years once clumps become congested.
propagation.methods: Divide just after foliage has died back, replanting offsets immediately.
propagation.tips: The easiest and most common way most gardeners ever propagate daffodils.
propagation.tracker: Propagation tracker coming soon — for now, add dates, method and result in your notes.

pollinator.value: An important early-season nectar source for bees emerging in spring when few other flowers are available yet.

timeline.Pruning: Deadhead after flowering; never cut foliage early

gallery: Naturalised drift in a border or lawn

GK-DAF-001.weeklyFact: Daffodils contain a natural toxin called lycorine, which is exactly why deer, squirrels and rabbits generally leave them alone.
weeklyFacts: Daffodils contain a natural toxin called lycorine, which is exactly why deer, squirrels and rabbits generally leave them alone.
A single daffodil bulb, left undisturbed, can naturalise into a large flowering clump over just a few years without any help.
GK-DAF-001.pollinatorFact: Daffodils are one of the most important early-spring nectar sources for bees just emerging from winter, when very little else is in flower.

GK-SNO-001.name: Snowdrop
GK-SNO-001.subtitle: The very first bulb of the gardening year, often flowering through snow.

quickCare.Water: Water at planting; otherwise relies on natural winter and spring rainfall
quickCare.Light: Part shade, ideally under deciduous trees or shrubs
quickCare.Feeding: No feeding needed in reasonable soil
quickCare.Pruning: Deadhead not necessary; leave foliage to die back naturally after flowering

GK-SNO-001.notes: Best planted 'in the green' (as growing plants just after flowering) rather than as dry bulbs, which establish far less reliably.
GK-SNO-001.medium: Peat-free compost with added leaf mould for containers.

seasonalActions.Spring: Flowers finish; foliage continues photosynthesising to recharge the bulb.
seasonalActions.Summer: Foliage dies back completely — this is normal, not a problem.
seasonalActions.Autumn: Dormant underground.
seasonalActions.Winter: Flowers emerge, sometimes through light snow, giving the plant its name.

problems.name: Grey mould in wet weather
problems.fix: Usually not serious; improve airflow in very congested clumps if it recurs.

problems.name: Slow to establish from dry bulbs
problems.fix: Plant 'in the green' (as leafy plants, just after flowering) instead for much better establishment.

propagation.season: Divide congested clumps 'in the green,' just after flowering finishes.
propagation.methods: Replant divisions immediately — snowdrop bulbs dislike drying out.
propagation.tips: One of the few bulbs best propagated as growing plants rather than dry bulbs.
propagation.tracker: Propagation tracker coming soon — for now, add dates, method and result in your notes.

pollinator.value: An important very-early nectar source for any bees or other insects active during mild spells in the depths of winter.

timeline.Seeding: N/A — grown from bulbs, best divided in the green
timeline.Pruning: No pruning needed; let foliage die back naturally

gallery: Flower close-up, ideally with snow or frost

GK-SNO-001.weeklyFact: Snowdrops produce a small amount of natural antifreeze-like compound in their cell sap, part of how they manage to flower through frost and snow.
weeklyFacts: Snowdrops produce a small amount of natural antifreeze-like compound in their cell sap, part of how they manage to flower through frost and snow.
Some rare snowdrop varieties ('galanthophile' collector plants) have sold at auction for hundreds of pounds for a single bulb.
GK-SNO-001.pollinatorFact: Snowdrops are a genuinely valuable very-early nectar source for any bees or other pollinators active on mild days in the depths of winter, when almost nothing else is flowering.

GK-ALL-001.name: Ornamental Allium
GK-ALL-001.subtitle: Striking spherical purple flowerheads on tall bare stems, beloved by bees.

quickCare.Water: Water at planting; otherwise reasonably drought-tolerant once established
quickCare.Light: Full sun
quickCare.Feeding: Light feed at planting; not a hungry plant
quickCare.Pruning: No pruning; leave seed heads standing for structure through summer and autumn

GK-ALL-001.notes: The dried seed heads are as ornamental as the flowers — leave them standing rather than cutting back for extra weeks of garden interest.
GK-ALL-001.soil: Free-draining soil; dislikes waterlogging, especially in winter.
GK-ALL-001.medium: Peat-free compost with added grit for containers.

seasonalActions.Spring: Flower spikes emerge and open into spherical flowerheads.
seasonalActions.Summer: Flowers fade to attractive dried seed heads; leave standing.
seasonalActions.Autumn: Foliage (often untidy by now) dies back; bulbs are dormant.
seasonalActions.Winter: Bulbs are fully dormant underground; no care needed.

problems.name: Foliage dying back untidily before flowering finishes
problems.fix: Normal for this genus; plant later-emerging perennials nearby to disguise the fading leaves.

problems.name: Bulb rot in waterlogged soil
problems.fix: Improve drainage significantly, especially over winter dormancy.

propagation.season: Bulbs naturally produce small offset bulbs that can be lifted and divided every few years.
propagation.methods: Divide once foliage has died back completely.
propagation.tips: Simple and reliable, though slower-multiplying than daffodils.
propagation.tracker: Propagation tracker coming soon — for now, add dates, method and result in your notes.

pollinator.value: An outstanding bee plant — the spherical flowerhead is made of dozens of small individual flowers, each a landing pad for a different visit.

timeline.Pruning: Leave seed heads standing after flowering

photoPlan.hero: Clear whole-plant photo of Ornamental Allium.
gallery: Whole plant showing height in a border

GK-ALL-001.weeklyFact: An allium flowerhead is actually a cluster of dozens of small star-shaped flowers packed into a single sphere, not one large bloom.
weeklyFacts: An allium flowerhead is actually a cluster of dozens of small star-shaped flowers packed into a single sphere, not one large bloom.
Ornamental allium is a close relative of onions, garlic and chives, all in the same genus, though grown purely for its flowers rather than to eat.
GK-ALL-001.pollinatorFact: Ornamental allium's spherical flowerheads are made up of dozens of individual small flowers, making a single plant capable of hosting many bee visits in one flowering.

GK-HYA-001.name: Hyacinth
GK-HYA-001.subtitle: Intensely fragrant spring bulb, equally at home in a border or an indoor bowl.

quickCare.Water: Water at planting; if grown indoors in a bowl, keep compost just moist, never waterlogged
quickCare.Light: Full sun outdoors; bright light if grown indoors
quickCare.Feeding: Light feed after flowering if planning to keep the bulb for next year
quickCare.Pruning: Deadhead after flowering; leave foliage to die back naturally

GK-HYA-001.notes: Indoor 'forced' hyacinth bulbs are usually spent after one indoor display — plant them out in the garden afterwards rather than discarding, where they can recover and flower again in future years.
GK-HYA-001.medium: Bulb fibre or free-draining compost for indoor bowls; peat-free compost with grit for outdoor containers.

seasonalActions.Spring: Flowers open with strong fragrance; deadhead as they fade.
seasonalActions.Summer: Foliage dies back naturally after flowering.
seasonalActions.Autumn: Plant new bulbs from September for next spring.
seasonalActions.Winter: Bulbs are dormant; indoor 'forced' bulbs need a cold, dark period first if starting from scratch.

problems.name: Bulb rot in waterlogged compost
problems.fix: Ensure sharp drainage, especially for bulbs grown in bowls without drainage holes.

problems.name: Flopping flower spikes
problems.fix: Common in forced indoor bulbs grown too warm too fast; keep cooler and provide some support if needed.

propagation.season: Bulbs produce small offsets over time, though multiplication is slower than daffodils.
propagation.methods: Divide congested clumps once foliage has died back.
propagation.tips: Most gardeners simply buy fresh bulbs each year rather than relying on division.
propagation.tracker: Propagation tracker coming soon — for now, add dates, method and result in your notes.

pollinator.value: Strongly fragrant flowers are a good early-season draw for bees on mild spring days.

timeline.Seeding: Plant bulbs Sep-Nov, or pre-chilled for indoor forcing
timeline.Flowering: Mar-Apr outdoors, earlier if forced indoors
timeline.Pruning: Deadhead after flowering

gallery: Whole plant in a border or bowl

GK-HYA-001.weeklyFact: A hyacinth's intense fragrance comes from an unusually high concentration of scent compounds packed into a relatively small flower spike.
weeklyFacts: A hyacinth's intense fragrance comes from an unusually high concentration of scent compounds packed into a relatively small flower spike.
'Forced' hyacinth bulbs sold for indoor winter displays have been deliberately tricked with a cold treatment to flower months ahead of their natural outdoor schedule.
GK-HYA-001.pollinatorFact: Hyacinth's strongly fragrant flowers are a useful early-season nectar source for bees active on mild spring days, alongside daffodils and crocus.

GK-CRC-001.name: Crocus
GK-CRC-001.subtitle: Tiny, brilliant, among the very first flowers of the gardening year.

quickCare.Water: Water at planting; otherwise relies on natural rainfall
quickCare.Light: Full sun — flowers close on dull days and open again in sunshine
quickCare.Feeding: No feeding needed in reasonable soil
quickCare.Pruning: No pruning; if naturalising in a lawn, don't mow until foliage has died back naturally

GK-CRC-001.notes: Genuinely one of the best bulbs for naturalising in grass — corms multiply readily and the foliage dies back early enough to allow normal mowing to resume.
GK-CRC-001.medium: Peat-free compost with added grit for containers.

seasonalActions.Spring: Flowers open in sunshine, often among the first colour of the year.
seasonalActions.Summer: Foliage dies back; safe to resume mowing over naturalised patches.
seasonalActions.Autumn: Plant new corms from September.
seasonalActions.Winter: Corms are fully dormant underground.

problems.name: Squirrels and mice digging up corms
problems.fix: Cover freshly planted areas with mesh until shoots appear, especially in gardens with a known problem.

problems.name: Corm rot in waterlogged soil
problems.fix: Improve drainage; crocus dislikes sitting wet, especially over summer dormancy.

propagation.season: Corms naturally produce small cormlets that can be lifted and divided every few years.
propagation.methods: Divide once foliage has died back completely.
propagation.tips: One of the easiest bulbs to naturalise and spread with minimal effort.
propagation.tracker: Propagation tracker coming soon — for now, add dates, method and result in your notes.

pollinator.value: An essential very-early nectar and pollen source — crocus flowers are often the first significant food available to bees emerging from winter hibernation.

timeline.Flowering: Feb-Mar, sometimes earlier in mild years
timeline.Pruning: Don't mow naturalised patches until foliage dies back

GK-CRC-001.weeklyFact: Crocus flowers physically close up on cloudy days and at night, only opening fully in direct sunlight — a response to temperature as much as light.
weeklyFacts: Crocus flowers physically close up on cloudy days and at night, only opening fully in direct sunlight — a response to temperature as much as light.
Saffron, one of the world's most expensive spices, comes from the stigmas of a specific autumn-flowering crocus species, Crocus sativus.
GK-CRC-001.pollinatorFact: Crocus is one of the most important very-early pollen and nectar sources of the whole year, often providing the first significant meal for bees just emerging from winter hibernation.

GK-BLU-001.name: Bluebell
GK-BLU-001.subtitle: Native woodland bulb that carpets shaded ground in vivid blue every spring.

quickCare.Water: Water at planting; otherwise relies on natural rainfall in its shaded woodland setting
quickCare.Light: Part to full shade, ideally under deciduous trees
quickCare.Feeding: No feeding needed in reasonable woodland-style soil
quickCare.Pruning: No pruning; let foliage die back naturally, which feeds the bulb for next year

GK-BLU-001.notes: Only plant true native English bluebells (Hyacinthoides non-scripta), not the more vigorous Spanish bluebell, which hybridises with and threatens native populations in the wild.
GK-BLU-001.medium: Peat-free compost with added leaf mould for any container growing, though this species prefers open ground.

seasonalActions.Spring: Flowers open in a vivid blue carpet under bare or newly leafing trees.
seasonalActions.Summer: Foliage dies back completely as tree canopy closes overhead.
seasonalActions.Autumn: Dormant underground beneath fallen leaves.
seasonalActions.Winter: Fully dormant; the leaf litter above acts as natural winter protection.

problems.name: Slow to establish from dry bulbs
problems.fix: Plant 'in the green' (as leafy plants, just after flowering) for much better establishment, similar to snowdrops.

problems.name: Hybridisation with Spanish bluebell
problems.fix: Avoid planting Spanish or hybrid bluebells nearby if preserving native English bluebell character matters to you.

propagation.season: Divide congested clumps 'in the green,' just after flowering, similar to snowdrops.
propagation.methods: Very slow to establish from seed — division of existing clumps is far more practical.
propagation.tips: Best left undisturbed once established; a mature bluebell wood can take years to develop.
propagation.tracker: Propagation tracker coming soon — for now, add dates, method and result in your notes.

pollinator.value: An important spring nectar source for woodland bees and other early pollinators, especially in shaded areas with fewer alternative flowers available.

timeline.Seeding: N/A — best divided in the green after flowering
timeline.Pruning: No pruning; let foliage die back naturally

gallery: Carpet of flowers in woodland setting
Woodland scene with bluebells and trees

GK-BLU-001.weeklyFact: English bluebell woods are considered a strong indicator of ancient woodland, since the plant spreads and establishes extremely slowly over many decades.
weeklyFacts: English bluebell woods are considered a strong indicator of ancient woodland, since the plant spreads and establishes extremely slowly over many decades.
Around half of the world's bluebells are found in the UK, making native bluebell woods a genuinely significant part of British woodland ecology.
GK-BLU-001.pollinatorFact: Bluebells are an important spring nectar source in shaded woodland settings, where early-flying bees have relatively few other flower choices available before the tree canopy closes overhead.

GK-HEB-001.name: Hebe
GK-HEB-001.subtitle: Neat, reliable evergreen shrub with small flower spikes over a long season.

quickCare.Water: Water while establishing; drought-tolerant once settled
quickCare.Light: Full sun to light shade
quickCare.Feeding: Light spring feed if growth is poor; not a hungry plant
quickCare.Pruning: Light trim after flowering to keep a neat shape; avoid cutting into old bare wood

GK-HEB-001.notes: Genuinely good for coastal or exposed gardens — many hebe varieties tolerate salt-laden wind better than most other flowering evergreens.
GK-HEB-001.medium: Peat-free compost with reasonable drainage for pots.

seasonalActions.Spring: Trim lightly to shape once the risk of hard frost has passed.
seasonalActions.Summer: Flowers over a long season on many varieties; deadhead if tidiness matters.
seasonalActions.Autumn: Light trim if needed; avoid cutting late enough to trigger tender new growth before winter.
seasonalActions.Winter: Some varieties are more frost-tender than others; check the specific variety's hardiness.

problems.name: Leaf spot in humid weather
problems.fix: Remove affected leaves and improve airflow between plants.

problems.name: Frost damage on tender varieties
problems.fix: Choose hardier varieties in colder gardens, or provide winter protection for borderline-hardy types.

propagation.season: Semi-ripe cuttings in summer root readily in a gritty mix — one of the easier shrubs to propagate this way.
propagation.methods: Softwood cuttings in late spring are also a reliable option.
propagation.tips: A good beginner shrub-propagation project thanks to a high success rate.
propagation.tracker: Propagation tracker coming soon — for now, add dates, method and result in your notes.

pollinator.value: Long flowering spikes on many varieties are popular with bees and butterflies over an extended summer season.

timeline.Pruning: Light trim after flowering, avoiding old wood

GK-HEB-001.weeklyFact: Hebe is named after the Greek goddess of youth, reflecting its evergreen, ever-fresh appearance.
weeklyFacts: Hebe is named after the Greek goddess of youth, reflecting its evergreen, ever-fresh appearance.
Nearly all hebe species originate from New Zealand, making it one of relatively few widely grown garden shrubs from that specific region.
GK-HEB-001.pollinatorFact: Hebe's long flowering spikes, especially on smaller-flowered varieties, are popular with bees and butterflies over an unusually extended summer season.

GK-WEI-001.name: Weigela
GK-WEI-001.subtitle: Arching deciduous shrub smothered in trumpet flowers in late spring.

quickCare.Water: Water while establishing; drought-tolerant once settled
quickCare.Light: Full sun to light shade for the best flowering
quickCare.Feeding: Light spring feed if growth or flowering is poor
quickCare.Pruning: Prune immediately after flowering, removing a proportion of old stems at the base to renew the shrub

GK-WEI-001.notes: Prune right after flowering, not in winter — winter pruning removes the flower buds that formed the previous summer, losing that year's display.
GK-WEI-001.medium: Peat-free compost with added organic matter for pots.

seasonalActions.Spring: Flowers open in a mass of trumpet-shaped blooms.
seasonalActions.Summer: Prune immediately after flowering finishes, removing some old stems at the base.
seasonalActions.Autumn: New growth from summer pruning matures and sets next year's flower buds.
seasonalActions.Winter: Deciduous and dormant; fully hardy in most gardens.

problems.name: Aphids on new growth
problems.fix: Wash off with water or encourage ladybirds; rarely serious.

problems.name: No flowers
problems.fix: Almost always pruned at the wrong time (winter, removing flower buds) — always prune right after flowering instead.

propagation.season: Softwood cuttings in early summer root reasonably well.
propagation.methods: Semi-ripe cuttings in late summer are a slightly slower but reliable alternative.
propagation.tips: A moderately easy shrub to propagate from cuttings with basic rooting hormone and a gritty mix.
propagation.tracker: Propagation tracker coming soon — for now, add dates, method and result in your notes.

pollinator.value: Tubular flowers are well suited to bees, particularly bumblebees able to access the trumpet shape.

timeline.Pruning: Prune immediately after flowering, not in winter

GK-WEI-001.weeklyFact: Weigela was named after German scientist Christian Ehrenfried von Weigel, and is a genus of around 10 species originally from East Asia.
weeklyFacts: Weigela was named after German scientist Christian Ehrenfried von Weigel, and is a genus of around 10 species originally from East Asia.
Because weigela sets its flower buds on the previous year's growth, pruning timing genuinely determines whether you get a full flower display or almost none.
GK-WEI-001.pollinatorFact: Weigela's tubular spring flowers are well suited to bumblebees, which are heavy and strong enough to access the trumpet shape efficiently.

GK-FOR-001.name: Forsythia
GK-FOR-001.subtitle: Vivid yellow flowers smother bare branches right at the start of spring.

quickCare.Water: Water while establishing; drought-tolerant once settled
quickCare.Light: Full sun to light shade for the best flowering
quickCare.Feeding: Light spring feed after flowering if growth is poor
quickCare.Pruning: Prune immediately after flowering; can be cut back hard as it regrows vigorously

GK-FOR-001.notes: One of the most reliable 'first sign of spring' shrubs — flowers appear on bare wood before the leaves even emerge.
GK-FOR-001.medium: Peat-free compost with added organic matter for pots.

seasonalActions.Spring: Vivid yellow flowers open on bare branches before leaves appear.
seasonalActions.Summer: Prune immediately after flowering finishes to shape and renew growth.
seasonalActions.Autumn: New growth from the spring prune matures and sets next year's flower buds.
seasonalActions.Winter: Deciduous and dormant; fully hardy and undemanding.

problems.name: No flowers
problems.fix: Almost always pruned at the wrong time (winter, removing flower buds) — always prune right after flowering instead.

problems.name: Overly vigorous, congested growth
problems.fix: Responds well to hard renovation pruning right after flowering if it's outgrown its space.

propagation.season: Hardwood cuttings in autumn or winter root very reliably, even for beginners.
propagation.methods: Softwood cuttings in early summer are also an option.
propagation.tips: One of the easiest shrubs in this library to propagate from cuttings.
propagation.tracker: Propagation tracker coming soon — for now, add dates, method and result in your notes.

pollinator.value: Early flowers provide a useful nectar source for the first bees active in early spring, though the tubular shape limits access for some smaller insects.

timeline.Pruning: Prune hard immediately after flowering

GK-FOR-001.weeklyFact: Forsythia flowers open before the leaves specifically to maximise visibility to early pollinators against a still-bare landscape.
weeklyFacts: Forsythia flowers open before the leaves specifically to maximise visibility to early pollinators against a still-bare landscape.
Forsythia is one of the most reliable shrubs for hardwood cuttings — a cut stem pushed straight into open ground in winter will often root with no special treatment at all.
GK-FOR-001.pollinatorFact: Forsythia's early flowers are a useful nectar source for the first bees becoming active in early spring, at a time when relatively few other shrubs are yet in bloom.

GK-LIL-001.name: Lilac
GK-LIL-001.subtitle: Deliciously fragrant spring shrub, a cottage garden classic for generations.

quickCare.Water: Water while establishing; drought-tolerant once settled
quickCare.Light: Full sun for the best flowering — shade significantly reduces bloom
quickCare.Feeding: Light spring feed if flowering is poor
quickCare.Pruning: Deadhead spent flower panicles; prune immediately after flowering if size needs controlling

GK-LIL-001.notes: Needs a genuinely sunny spot to flower well — a lilac struggling in shade is one of the most common 'why won't it flower' questions, and the fix is almost always more light.
GK-LIL-001.medium: Peat-free compost with added organic matter for pots, though lilac generally prefers open ground long-term.

seasonalActions.Spring: Flower panicles open with strong, classic fragrance.
seasonalActions.Summer: Deadhead spent flowers; prune immediately after if reshaping is needed.
seasonalActions.Autumn: New growth sets next year's flower buds.
seasonalActions.Winter: Deciduous and fully hardy; needs no winter protection.

problems.name: Poor or no flowering
problems.fix: Almost always insufficient sun; move to a sunnier spot or manage overhanging shade if possible.

problems.name: Powdery mildew on leaves in late summer
problems.fix: Usually cosmetic by that point in the season; improve airflow for future years.

propagation.season: Softwood cuttings in early summer can be tricky to root but are worth attempting for named varieties.
propagation.methods: Suckers (shoots from the base) can sometimes be removed with roots attached and potted separately.
propagation.tips: Grafted named varieties are usually bought as young plants rather than propagated at home.
propagation.tracker: Propagation tracker coming soon — for now, add dates, method and result in your notes.

pollinator.value: Fragrant flower panicles are a good nectar source for bees and butterflies during their relatively brief but intense flowering period.

timeline.Pruning: Deadhead after flowering; prune immediately after if needed

GK-LIL-001.weeklyFact: Lilac's intense fragrance has made it one of the most commonly referenced flowers in perfumery, despite being notoriously difficult to extract true lilac scent from directly.
weeklyFacts: Lilac's intense fragrance has made it one of the most commonly referenced flowers in perfumery, despite being notoriously difficult to extract true lilac scent from directly.
A well-sited lilac can live and flower reliably for well over 50 years with minimal care beyond a sunny spot and the occasional post-flowering tidy.
GK-LIL-001.pollinatorFact: Lilac's fragrant flower panicles are a strong short-term draw for bees and butterflies during its relatively brief but intense spring flowering window.

GK-CHO-001.name: Choisya
GK-CHO-001.subtitle: Glossy evergreen shrub with fragrant white flowers, often blooming twice a year.

quickCare.Water: Water while establishing; drought-tolerant once settled
quickCare.Light: Full sun to part shade
quickCare.Feeding: Light spring feed if growth is poor; not a hungry plant
quickCare.Pruning: Trim to shape after the main spring flush; avoid hard pruning into old bare wood

GK-CHO-001.notes: One of the most reliably low-maintenance evergreen shrubs available — glossy year-round foliage plus two flowering flushes for very little work.
GK-CHO-001.medium: Peat-free compost with added organic matter for pots.

seasonalActions.Spring: Main flush of fragrant white flowers opens.
seasonalActions.Summer: Trim lightly to shape after the spring flush finishes.
seasonalActions.Autumn: A second, lighter flush of flowers often appears.
seasonalActions.Winter: Evergreen and generally hardy, though very hard frost can damage foliage in exposed spots.

problems.name: Frost damage to foliage in exposed sites
problems.fix: Choose a sheltered spot in colder gardens, or accept some winter leaf browning, which usually recovers.

problems.name: Leaf drop after transplanting
problems.fix: Normal transplant shock; keep well watered while it re-establishes.

propagation.season: Semi-ripe cuttings in summer root reasonably reliably in a gritty mix.
propagation.methods: A moderately easy shrub to propagate, though patience is needed as growth is fairly slow.
propagation.tips: Layering a low branch is a slower but very reliable alternative method.
propagation.tracker: Propagation tracker coming soon — for now, add dates, method and result in your notes.

pollinator.value: Fragrant flowers are popular with bees, and the double flowering habit (spring plus a lighter second flush) extends the useful nectar window.

timeline.Flowering: Apr-May, often with a second flush in autumn
timeline.Pruning: Trim after the main spring flush, avoiding old wood

gallery: Second autumn flush if it occurs

GK-CHO-001.weeklyFact: Choisya's common name, Mexican orange blossom, comes from both its native range and a citrus-like fragrance in the flowers, despite being unrelated to true oranges.
weeklyFacts: Choisya's common name, Mexican orange blossom, comes from both its native range and a citrus-like fragrance in the flowers, despite being unrelated to true oranges.
Its glossy, aromatic leaves release a distinct scent when crushed, a trait shared with several other plants in its wider family.
GK-CHO-001.pollinatorFact: Choisya's fragrant flowers, often appearing in two flushes across the year, provide a useful and unusually extended nectar window for bees.

GK-BIR-001.name: Silver Birch
GK-BIR-001.subtitle: Elegant, fast-growing native tree with striking white bark and light, airy canopy.

quickCare.Water: Water well for the first two years while establishing; established trees rely on natural rainfall
quickCare.Light: Full sun to part shade
quickCare.Feeding: No feeding usually needed once established in reasonable soil
quickCare.Pruning: Prune only when fully dormant in mid-to-late winter — birch 'bleeds' sap heavily if cut in spring

GK-BIR-001.notes: Never prune birch in spring or summer — cut stems bleed sap dramatically at that time of year; always prune in deep winter dormancy instead.
GK-BIR-001.soil: Adaptable to most soils, though prefers slightly acidic, well-drained ground.
GK-BIR-001.medium: Not typically grown in containers long-term; young trees can start in large pots before planting out.

seasonalActions.Spring: Catkins appear before leaves fully unfurl.
seasonalActions.Summer: Full leaf canopy provides light, dappled shade.
seasonalActions.Autumn: Leaves turn a clear yellow before falling — a genuine autumn highlight.
seasonalActions.Winter: Bare white bark is most visible and striking, especially against dark winter skies.

problems.name: Bracket fungi on mature or stressed trees
problems.fix: A sign of internal decay in older trees; consult an arborist if structural safety is a concern.

problems.name: Aphid honeydew dripping from canopy
problems.fix: Common and mostly cosmetic; supports a healthy food chain of aphid predators beneath the tree.

propagation.season: Grown from seed, which needs a cold period (stratification) to germinate well.
propagation.methods: Named ornamental varieties are usually grafted rather than grown from seed.
propagation.tips: Most gardeners buy young trees rather than growing birch from seed at home.
propagation.tracker: Propagation tracker coming soon — for now, add dates, method and result in your notes.

pollinator.value: Catkins are wind-pollinated rather than insect-pollinated, but birch trees support an exceptionally large number of insect species that in turn feed birds and other wildlife.

timeline.Seeding: N/A — usually bought as young trees
timeline.Pruning: Prune only in deep winter dormancy

photoPlan.hero: Clear whole-plant photo of Silver Birch.
gallery: Whole tree showing white bark and canopy shape

GK-BIR-001.weeklyFact: Silver birch bark naturally peels in thin white papery layers, a genuine adaptation that helps the tree shed lichen and moss and reflect excess sunlight.
weeklyFacts: Silver birch bark naturally peels in thin white papery layers, a genuine adaptation that helps the tree shed lichen and moss and reflect excess sunlight.
A mature birch canopy is thought to support several hundred species of insect, more than almost any other native tree except oak.
GK-BIR-001.pollinatorFact: Birch catkins are wind-pollinated rather than relying on insects, but the tree as a whole is an exceptionally important host for hundreds of insect species that feed birds and other wildlife through the food chain.

GK-APP-001.name: Apple Tree
GK-APP-001.subtitle: Classic fruiting tree — needs a pollination partner and a bit of yearly pruning, but rewards for decades.

quickCare.Water: Water well in the first couple of years and during dry spells while fruit is swelling
quickCare.Light: Full sun for the best fruit production
quickCare.Feeding: Balanced feed in spring; mulch with well-rotted organic matter
quickCare.Pruning: Prune while dormant in winter for most varieties, removing crossing, dead or crowded branches

GK-APP-001.notes: Most apple varieties need a different, compatible variety flowering at the same time nearby for good pollination and fruit set — check your variety's pollination group before assuming a single tree will crop well alone.
GK-APP-001.medium: Not typically container-grown long-term except on very dwarfing rootstocks in large pots.

seasonalActions.Spring: Blossom opens; a late frost at this stage can damage the crop for the year.
seasonalActions.Summer: Thin developing fruit if very heavy for larger, better-quality apples; water in dry spells.
seasonalActions.Autumn: Harvest as fruit ripens; varieties differ widely in timing.
seasonalActions.Winter: Prune while fully dormant, avoiding periods of hard frost.

problems.name: Apple scab
problems.fix: Dark scabby patches on leaves and fruit; rake up and dispose of fallen leaves in autumn to reduce next year's spores.

problems.name: Codling moth
problems.fix: Maggots inside the fruit core; pheromone traps and good orchard hygiene help reduce numbers over a few seasons.

problems.name: Poor fruit set
problems.fix: Usually a pollination partner issue or late frost damage to blossom; check pollination group compatibility.

propagation.season: Grafting onto a specific rootstock is the standard method, since apples don't come true from seed and rootstock choice controls eventual tree size.
propagation.methods: Most gardeners buy a grafted young tree rather than attempting to graft at home.
propagation.tips: Seed-grown apples are a fun experiment but will produce an unpredictable, usually inferior fruit compared to the parent.
propagation.tracker: Propagation tracker coming soon — for now, add dates, method and result in your notes.

pollinator.value: Apple blossom is a major early-to-mid spring nectar source, and good pollination directly determines the size of the eventual harvest.

timeline.Seeding: N/A — grown from grafted young trees
timeline.Pruning: Prune while dormant in winter

photoPlan.hero: Clear whole-plant photo of Apple Tree.

GK-APP-001.weeklyFact: A single well-established apple tree, if well pollinated, can produce well over 100kg of fruit in a good year, depending on variety and rootstock.
weeklyFacts: A single well-established apple tree, if well pollinated, can produce well over 100kg of fruit in a good year, depending on variety and rootstock.
Apples genuinely don't come true from seed — planting a pip from a shop-bought apple will grow into a real apple tree, but almost certainly not one that produces fruit anything like the parent.
GK-APP-001.pollinatorFact: Apple blossom is a major spring nectar and pollen source for bees, and the tree is entirely dependent on good insect pollination from a compatible partner variety for a worthwhile fruit harvest.

GK-MAG-001.name: Magnolia
GK-MAG-001.subtitle: Dramatic, large goblet-shaped spring flowers on an ancient, primitive flowering tree.

quickCare.Water: Water well, especially newly planted trees and those in pots — dislikes drying out
quickCare.Light: Full sun to part shade, sheltered from strong wind and late frost
quickCare.Feeding: Light spring feed with an ericaceous or balanced fertiliser
quickCare.Pruning: Minimal pruning needed; if required, do it right after flowering to avoid removing next year's buds

GK-MAG-001.notes: Flower buds are famously vulnerable to a late frost after a mild spell — a sheltered spot away from early morning sun (which thaws frozen buds too fast) can save a whole season's display.
GK-MAG-001.soil: Fertile, humus-rich, moisture-retentive but well-drained soil, ideally slightly acidic.
GK-MAG-001.medium: Ericaceous peat-free compost for pot-grown young specimens.

seasonalActions.Spring: Large goblet-shaped flowers open, often before leaves — extremely vulnerable to late frost at this stage.
seasonalActions.Summer: Full leaf canopy develops after flowering finishes.
seasonalActions.Autumn: Leaves may show some autumn colour depending on variety.
seasonalActions.Winter: Fully dormant; protect young or pot-grown specimens' root zones from hard freezing.

problems.name: Frost damage to flower buds
problems.fix: The most common magnolia disappointment; choose a sheltered site away from early morning sun to reduce the risk.

problems.name: Slow to establish
problems.fix: Normal for the first couple of years; magnolias are worth the patience once established.

propagation.season: Layering a low branch into the soil is the most reliable amateur method, though it can take a year or more to root.
propagation.methods: Semi-ripe cuttings are possible but can be slow and inconsistent to root.
propagation.tips: Most gardeners buy an established young tree rather than propagating magnolia at home.
propagation.tracker: Propagation tracker coming soon — for now, add dates, method and result in your notes.

pollinator.value: Magnolia is pollinated primarily by beetles rather than bees, an evolutionary holdover from before bees existed, though some bee visits still occur.

timeline.Pruning: Minimal; if needed, prune right after flowering

gallery: Bare winter branch structure with buds

GK-MAG-001.weeklyFact: Magnolias are one of the most ancient flowering plant genera alive today, evolving before bees existed — their tough, simple flower structure is specifically adapted to survive being pollinated by beetles.
weeklyFacts: Magnolias are one of the most ancient flowering plant genera alive today, evolving before bees existed — their tough, simple flower structure is specifically adapted to survive being pollinated by beetles.
Because magnolia flowers evolved for beetle pollination, they lack true nectar, offering pollen and a protein-rich flower structure as the reward instead.
GK-MAG-001.pollinatorFact: Magnolia is pollinated primarily by beetles, an evolutionary trait from before bees existed on Earth, though its flowers still receive occasional visits from bees drawn to the abundant pollen.

GK-ROW-001.name: Rowan
GK-ROW-001.subtitle: Hardy native tree with white spring blossom and vivid orange-red autumn berries for birds.

quickCare.Water: Water well for the first couple of years while establishing; established trees are drought-tolerant
quickCare.Light: Full sun to part shade
quickCare.Feeding: No feeding usually needed once established
quickCare.Pruning: Prune while dormant in winter if shaping is needed; naturally forms a neat shape with little intervention

GK-ROW-001.notes: One of the best small-to-medium native trees for wildlife value — blossom for pollinators in spring, then a heavy crop of berries that birds strip within days in autumn.
GK-ROW-001.soil: Adaptable to most soils, including poor and exposed sites.
GK-ROW-001.medium: Not typically container-grown long-term; young trees can start in large pots before planting out.

seasonalActions.Spring: Clusters of white blossom open.
seasonalActions.Summer: Berries begin to form and ripen through late summer.
seasonalActions.Autumn: Berries turn vivid orange-red and are quickly stripped by birds.
seasonalActions.Winter: Bare branch structure; fully hardy and undemanding.

problems.name: Fireblight
problems.fix: Can affect blossom and young shoots, causing a scorched appearance; prune out and destroy affected wood promptly, disinfecting tools between cuts.

problems.name: Rowan sawfly
problems.fix: Caterpillar-like larvae can strip leaves in some years; usually not serious enough to threaten an established tree.

propagation.season: Autumn, using stratified seed, or buy a young grafted/seed-grown tree
propagation.methods: Grown from seed, which needs a cold period (stratification) to break dormancy.
propagation.tips: Berries are sometimes fed to birds and the seed germinates naturally from droppings, which is roughly how it spreads in the wild.
propagation.tracker: Propagation tracker coming soon — for now, add dates, method and result in your notes.

pollinator.value: Blossom is a useful spring nectar source for bees and other insects, and the berries that follow are an important autumn food source for garden birds.

timeline.Seeding: N/A — usually bought as young trees, or grown from stratified seed
timeline.Harvest: Berries Aug-Oct, quickly taken by birds
timeline.Pruning: Prune while dormant in winter if needed

gallery: Birds feeding on berries if captured

GK-ROW-001.weeklyFact: Rowan was traditionally planted near houses in parts of Britain and Ireland in the belief it offered protection from evil — a folklore tradition still visible in old cottage garden plantings today.
weeklyFacts: Rowan was traditionally planted near houses in parts of Britain and Ireland in the belief it offered protection from evil — a folklore tradition still visible in old cottage garden plantings today.
A single rowan tree's autumn berry crop can be completely stripped by a flock of birds, such as fieldfares or waxwings, within just a day or two.
GK-ROW-001.pollinatorFact: Rowan's spring blossom is a useful nectar source for bees, and its heavy autumn berry crop is one of the most important native food sources for garden birds heading into winter.

GK-PEO-001.name: Peony
GK-PEO-001.subtitle: Lush, long-lived border perennial with huge, often fragrant early-summer blooms.

quickCare.Water: Water while establishing; drought-tolerant once settled, though appreciates water while budding
quickCare.Light: Full sun for the best flowering
quickCare.Feeding: Light spring feed; avoid rich nitrogen feed, which favours leaves over flowers
quickCare.Pruning: Cut foliage down to the ground once it dies back in autumn

GK-PEO-001.notes: Plant with the crown no more than 2-5cm below the soil surface — planting too deep is the single most common reason a peony fails to flower.
GK-PEO-001.medium: Not usually grown long-term in containers; needs a deep, stable root run.

seasonalActions.Spring: Shoots emerge fast; stake floppy varieties early before flowers are heavy with rain.
seasonalActions.Summer: Deadhead spent flowers; water in prolonged dry spells while establishing.
seasonalActions.Autumn: Foliage may show some autumn colour before dying back.
seasonalActions.Winter: Cut all foliage to the ground once it has died back, to reduce disease carried over on old leaves.

problems.name: No flowers ('blindness')
problems.fix: Almost always planted too deep; the crown should sit just at or barely below soil level.

problems.name: Botrytis (grey mould) on buds
problems.fix: Remove affected buds promptly and improve airflow around the plant.

propagation.season: Divide dormant crowns in autumn, ensuring each division has 3-5 healthy 'eyes' (buds).
propagation.methods: Peonies resent disturbance and can sulk for a year or two after division before flowering well again.
propagation.tips: Most gardeners simply buy an established plant and leave it undisturbed for decades rather than propagating.
propagation.tracker: Propagation tracker coming soon — for now, add dates, method and result in your notes.

pollinator.value: Large, pollen-rich flowers are popular with bees, especially single and semi-double varieties.

timeline.Seeding: N/A — grown from divisions or young plants
timeline.Harvest: Cut flowers in bud, just as colour shows
timeline.Pruning: Cut all foliage to the ground once it dies back in autumn

GK-PEO-001.weeklyFact: A well-sited peony can live and flower reliably in the same spot for 50 years or more, making it one of the longest-lived perennials in a typical garden.
weeklyFacts: A well-sited peony can live and flower reliably in the same spot for 50 years or more, making it one of the longest-lived perennials in a typical garden.
Peonies are famously visited by ants while in bud, drawn to a sugary secretion on the bud surface — the ants don't harm the plant and aren't necessary for it to flower.
GK-PEO-001.pollinatorFact: Large single and semi-double peony flowers offer bees generous access to pollen, though very full double forms can hide it almost entirely.

GK-DEL-001.name: Delphinium
GK-DEL-001.subtitle: Dramatic tall flower spires in vivid blues, a cottage garden showstopper.

quickCare.Water: Water deeply and regularly, especially in dry spells — shallow watering encourages weak roots
quickCare.Light: Full sun, sheltered from strong wind given the height of the flower spikes
quickCare.Feeding: Regular feed through the growing season; a hungry plant for a big display
quickCare.Pruning: Cut spent flower spikes down to encourage a smaller second flush; cut all growth to the ground in autumn

GK-DEL-001.notes: Stake early and thoroughly — a mature delphinium spike is tall and heavy, and a single strong gust can snap an unstaked plant at the base.
GK-DEL-001.medium: Not typically grown long-term in containers; prefers open ground with a deep root run.

seasonalActions.Spring: Fast new growth; stake early before spikes gain height and weight, and watch closely for slug damage.
seasonalActions.Summer: Deadhead spent spikes for a smaller second flush; water deeply in dry spells.
seasonalActions.Autumn: Cut all growth to the ground once it has died back.
seasonalActions.Winter: Dormant crown below ground; fully hardy in most gardens.

problems.name: Slugs and snails
problems.fix: The single biggest delphinium threat, especially on young spring shoots; use barriers or torchlight removal from the moment shoots appear.

problems.name: Powdery mildew late season
problems.fix: Improve airflow between plants and avoid overhead watering.

propagation.season: Basal cuttings taken in spring, just as new shoots emerge from the crown, root well in a gritty mix.
propagation.methods: Division of established clumps is possible but can set the plant back a season.
propagation.tips: Seed-grown plants are variable and slow to reach flowering size, so most gardeners buy young plants or take cuttings.
propagation.tracker: Propagation tracker coming soon — for now, add dates, method and result in your notes.

pollinator.value: An outstanding bee plant — the tall spires of tubular flowers are specifically well suited to bumblebees, which can be seen working up and down a single spike.

timeline.Flowering: Jun-Jul, sometimes a smaller second flush
timeline.Pruning: Cut all growth to the ground in autumn

gallery: Whole spike showing height in a border

GK-DEL-001.weeklyFact: A single delphinium flower spike can hold over 50 individual blooms, opening from the bottom of the spike upward over several weeks.
weeklyFacts: A single delphinium flower spike can hold over 50 individual blooms, opening from the bottom of the spike upward over several weeks.
Delphinium is toxic if eaten, and was historically used in very dilute, controlled preparations in traditional pest-control recipes.
GK-DEL-001.pollinatorFact: Delphinium's tall spires of tubular flowers are exceptionally well suited to bumblebees, which methodically work their way up and down each spike collecting nectar and pollen.

GK-LUP-001.name: Lupin
GK-LUP-001.subtitle: Tall, colourful spires that fix their own nitrogen and self-seed readily.

quickCare.Water: Water while establishing; reasonably drought-tolerant once settled
quickCare.Light: Full sun to light shade
quickCare.Feeding: Little feeding needed — lupins fix their own nitrogen from the air via root nodules
quickCare.Pruning: Deadhead spent spikes to prevent excessive self-seeding and encourage a smaller second flush

GK-LUP-001.notes: As a legume, lupin actually improves the soil it grows in by fixing nitrogen, unlike most other border perennials.
GK-LUP-001.soil: Free-draining, slightly acidic soil; dislikes chalky or waterlogged ground.
GK-LUP-001.medium: Not typically container-grown long-term; prefers open ground with room for a deep taproot.

seasonalActions.Spring: Fast new growth; watch closely for aphids on soft new shoots.
seasonalActions.Summer: Deadhead spent spikes for a smaller second flush.
seasonalActions.Autumn: Foliage may die back; leave some seed pods if self-seeding is wanted.
seasonalActions.Winter: Dormant crown below ground; fully hardy in most gardens.

problems.name: Lupin aphid
problems.fix: Large greyish aphids can cluster densely on stems in early summer; wash off with a strong jet of water or encourage ladybirds.

problems.name: Powdery mildew
problems.fix: Improve airflow between plants and avoid overhead watering in humid weather.

propagation.season: Self-seeds readily — often the simplest method is to let a few spikes set seed and self-sow.
propagation.methods: Basal cuttings in spring root reasonably well for named colour varieties.
propagation.tips: Seed benefits from nicking the tough seed coat or soaking overnight to speed germination.
propagation.tracker: Propagation tracker coming soon — for now, add dates, method and result in your notes.

pollinator.value: Lupin's pea-family flower shape is a textbook match for bumblebees, which trigger a small internal mechanism to release pollen as they land.

timeline.Pruning: Deadhead spent spikes; cut back in autumn

gallery: Seed pods if left to set seed

GK-LUP-001.weeklyFact: Lupin leaves famously hold perfect droplets of water after rain or dew, thanks to a water-repellent leaf surface — a genuinely striking garden photo opportunity.
weeklyFacts: Lupin leaves famously hold perfect droplets of water after rain or dew, thanks to a water-repellent leaf surface — a genuinely striking garden photo opportunity.
As a legume, lupin roots host bacteria that convert nitrogen from the air into a form the plant (and surrounding soil) can use, effectively fertilising itself.
GK-LUP-001.pollinatorFact: Lupin's pea-shaped flowers are a specific mechanical match for bumblebees, which trigger a small trip-lever action in the flower to release pollen onto their bodies as they land.

GK-PHL-001.name: Phlox
GK-PHL-001.subtitle: Fragrant, densely flowered late-summer perennial that draws in moths at dusk.

quickCare.Water: Water consistently, especially in dry spells — shallow, infrequent watering encourages powdery mildew
quickCare.Light: Full sun to part shade, with good airflow around the plant
quickCare.Feeding: Light spring feed if growth is poor
quickCare.Pruning: Deadhead spent flower clusters; cut all growth to the ground once it dies back in autumn

GK-PHL-001.notes: Airflow matters more than almost any other factor for phlox — crowd it and powdery mildew becomes almost guaranteed by late summer.
GK-PHL-001.medium: Peat-free compost with added organic matter for large containers, kept consistently moist.

seasonalActions.Spring: New growth emerges; thin congested clumps for better airflow if mildew has been a repeat problem.
seasonalActions.Summer: Water consistently and deadhead spent clusters.
seasonalActions.Autumn: Cut back once foliage dies down after flowering.
seasonalActions.Winter: Dormant; fully hardy in most gardens.

problems.name: Powdery mildew
problems.fix: The classic phlox problem, worse in crowded or poorly ventilated spots; thin plants for airflow and water at the base, not overhead.

problems.name: Eelworm (stem nematode)
problems.fix: Causes stunted, distorted growth; affected plants are best lifted and destroyed rather than treated, and avoid replanting phlox in the same spot.

propagation.season: Root cuttings in winter are a reliable way to propagate clean stock, especially useful if eelworm has been a problem with the parent plant.
propagation.methods: Division of healthy clumps in spring or autumn is the simpler everyday method.
propagation.tips: Basal cuttings in spring are also possible for a quick, simple increase.
propagation.tracker: Propagation tracker coming soon — for now, add dates, method and result in your notes.

pollinator.value: Strongly fragrant flower clusters are popular with bees during the day and moths at dusk, making phlox useful across a longer part of the day than many border perennials.

timeline.Seeding: N/A — grown from young plants or divisions
timeline.Pruning: Cut back once foliage dies down in autumn

gallery: Moths visiting at dusk if captured

GK-PHL-001.weeklyFact: Phlox's fragrance genuinely intensifies in the evening, an adaptation to attract moths once daytime pollinators have finished for the day.
weeklyFacts: Phlox's fragrance genuinely intensifies in the evening, an adaptation to attract moths once daytime pollinators have finished for the day.
The name Phlox comes from the Greek word for 'flame,' a reference to the vivid colour range of the original wild species.
GK-PHL-001.pollinatorFact: Phlox's fragrant flower clusters draw in both daytime bees and evening moths, giving it a genuinely extended pollinator window compared to most border perennials.

GK-RUD-001.name: Rudbeckia
GK-RUD-001.subtitle: Bold golden daisies with dark centres, flowering strongly right into autumn.

quickCare.Water: Water while establishing; reasonably drought-tolerant once settled
quickCare.Light: Full sun
quickCare.Feeding: Light spring feed if growth is poor; not a hungry plant
quickCare.Pruning: Deadhead to prolong flowering, or leave seed heads standing for birds and winter structure

GK-RUD-001.notes: One of the best value-for-effort late-season perennials — reliable, long-flowering colour right as many other border plants are finishing for the year.
GK-RUD-001.medium: Peat-free compost with reasonable moisture retention for containers.

seasonalActions.Spring: New growth emerges; divide congested clumps if needed.
seasonalActions.Summer: Deadhead regularly for continuous flowering through summer.
seasonalActions.Autumn: Flowering continues strongly; leave some seed heads standing for birds through winter.
seasonalActions.Winter: Leave seed heads standing over winter for structure and bird food, cut back in early spring instead.

problems.name: Powdery mildew late season
problems.fix: Improve airflow between plants; rarely serious enough to stop flowering.

problems.name: Slugs on young spring shoots
problems.fix: Protect emerging growth with barriers or torchlight removal.

propagation.season: Division of established clumps in spring or autumn is quick and reliable.
propagation.methods: Basal cuttings in spring are also a reasonable option.
propagation.tips: One of the easier perennials in this library to propagate and share.
propagation.tracker: Propagation tracker coming soon — for now, add dates, method and result in your notes.

pollinator.value: An excellent late-season nectar source for bees and butterflies, and the seed heads that follow are a valuable autumn and winter food source for finches and other birds.

timeline.Seeding: N/A — grown from young plants or divisions
timeline.Pruning: Leave seed heads for winter; cut back in early spring

gallery: Seed heads with birds feeding if captured

GK-RUD-001.weeklyFact: The dark central cone of a rudbeckia flower is made up of hundreds of tiny individual disc florets, each capable of producing its own seed.
weeklyFacts: The dark central cone of a rudbeckia flower is made up of hundreds of tiny individual disc florets, each capable of producing its own seed.
Goldfinches are frequently seen feeding directly on rudbeckia seed heads left standing over winter, making it a genuinely dual-purpose plant for pollinators and birds.
GK-RUD-001.pollinatorFact: Rudbeckia is an excellent late-season nectar source for bees and butterflies right as many other plants finish flowering, and its seed heads go on to feed finches and other birds through autumn and winter.

GK-POTL-001.name: Potentilla
GK-POTL-001.subtitle: Tough, undemanding shrub with small saucer flowers over an exceptionally long season.

quickCare.Water: Water while establishing; drought-tolerant once settled
quickCare.Light: Full sun for the best flowering
quickCare.Feeding: Little to no feeding needed — thrives on relative neglect
quickCare.Pruning: Light trim in early spring to keep a neat shape; can be cut back hard every few years to renovate

GK-POTL-001.notes: One of the longest continuous flowering seasons of any common garden shrub — often in bloom from late spring right through to the first frosts.
GK-POTL-001.soil: Any reasonable, well-drained soil — genuinely unfussy.
GK-POTL-001.medium: Peat-free compost with reasonable drainage for pots.

seasonalActions.Spring: Light trim to shape before new growth gets underway.
seasonalActions.Summer: Flowers continuously right through summer with minimal input.
seasonalActions.Autumn: Flowering often continues into autumn until the first frosts.
seasonalActions.Winter: Deciduous and fully hardy; needs no protection.

problems.name: Sparse flowering in shade
problems.fix: Move to a sunnier spot — flowering drops off noticeably without good light.

problems.name: Straggly, overgrown shape after some years
problems.fix: Responds well to being cut back hard in early spring every few years to rejuvenate.

propagation.season: Semi-ripe cuttings in summer root reasonably reliably in a gritty mix.
propagation.methods: Softwood cuttings in early summer are also an option.
propagation.tips: A fairly easy, forgiving shrub to propagate from cuttings.
propagation.tracker: Propagation tracker coming soon — for now, add dates, method and result in your notes.

pollinator.value: Simple, open saucer-shaped flowers are easily accessed by bees and hoverflies over an unusually long flowering season.

timeline.Pruning: Light trim in early spring; hard renovation every few years if needed

GK-POTL-001.weeklyFact: Potentilla's open, saucer-shaped flowers stay in bloom for an unusually long stretch of the year compared to almost any other hardy shrub — often five months or more.
weeklyFacts: Potentilla's open, saucer-shaped flowers stay in bloom for an unusually long stretch of the year compared to almost any other hardy shrub — often five months or more.
The genus name Potentilla comes from the Latin for 'powerful,' a reference to the plant's traditional use in herbal medicine rather than its garden toughness, though the name fits both.
GK-POTL-001.pollinatorFact: Potentilla's simple, open flowers are easy for bees and hoverflies to access, and its exceptionally long flowering season makes it a reliable nectar source across most of the growing year.

GK-SPI-002.name: Spiraea
GK-SPI-002.subtitle: Compact, easy-going shrub with flat flower clusters and often colourful new foliage.

quickCare.Water: Water while establishing; drought-tolerant once settled
quickCare.Light: Full sun to light shade
quickCare.Feeding: Light spring feed if growth is poor
quickCare.Pruning: Cut back hard in early spring, which encourages strong new flowering growth

GK-SPI-002.notes: Responds very well to a hard annual cut-back in early spring — unlike many shrubs, this genuinely improves rather than harms next season's display.
GK-SPI-002.medium: Peat-free compost with reasonable drainage for pots.

seasonalActions.Spring: Cut back hard before new growth gets underway — this shrub flowers on new wood.
seasonalActions.Summer: Flat flower clusters open on fresh growth.
seasonalActions.Autumn: Deadhead if tidiness matters; otherwise low maintenance through autumn.
seasonalActions.Winter: Deciduous and fully hardy; needs no protection.

problems.name: Aphids on new growth
problems.fix: Wash off with water or encourage ladybirds; rarely serious.

problems.name: Sparse flowering
problems.fix: Usually not cut back hard enough in spring — this variety flowers best on strong new growth after a firm annual prune.

propagation.season: Softwood cuttings in early summer root readily in a gritty mix.
propagation.methods: Semi-ripe cuttings in late summer are also a reliable option.
propagation.tips: One of the easier flowering shrubs to propagate from cuttings.
propagation.tracker: Propagation tracker coming soon — for now, add dates, method and result in your notes.
