pollinator.value: Flat flower clusters are easily accessed by bees and hoverflies, offering a good simple nectar source for smaller pollinator species in particular.

timeline.Pruning: Cut back hard in early spring before new growth starts

gallery: New spring foliage colour if variegated/coloured variety
Newly pruned shrub in early spring

GK-SPI-002.weeklyFact: Some spiraea varieties produce vividly orange or red new foliage in spring, giving two distinct seasons of colour interest from a single low-maintenance shrub.
weeklyFacts: Some spiraea varieties produce vividly orange or red new foliage in spring, giving two distinct seasons of colour interest from a single low-maintenance shrub.
Spiraea flowers on new wood, which is exactly why a hard early-spring cut-back improves rather than reduces the summer flower display.
GK-SPI-002.pollinatorFact: Spiraea's flat flower clusters give bees and hoverflies easy, open access to nectar and pollen, making it a reliable simple food source for smaller pollinator species.

GK-COT-001.name: Cotoneaster
GK-COT-001.subtitle: Tough, semi-evergreen shrub with small flowers followed by a heavy autumn berry crop for birds.

quickCare.Water: Water while establishing; drought-tolerant once settled
quickCare.Light: Full sun to part shade
quickCare.Feeding: No feeding usually needed once established
quickCare.Pruning: Light trim after flowering if shaping is needed; avoid cutting off developing berries

GK-COT-001.notes: A genuinely excellent low-effort wildlife shrub — small flowers for pollinators in summer, followed by a heavy berry crop that reliably feeds birds through autumn and winter.
GK-COT-001.soil: Any reasonable, well-drained soil — very unfussy.
GK-COT-001.medium: Peat-free compost with reasonable drainage for pots.

seasonalActions.Spring: Small flower clusters open.
seasonalActions.Summer: Berries begin forming; water in prolonged dry spells.
seasonalActions.Autumn: Berries ripen to red/orange and are gradually taken by birds.
seasonalActions.Winter: Semi-evergreen; retains some foliage through most winters.

problems.name: Fireblight
problems.fix: Can affect blossom and shoots, causing a scorched appearance; prune out and destroy affected wood promptly, disinfecting tools between cuts.

problems.name: Cotoneaster webber moth
problems.fix: Caterpillars can web up and skeletonise leaves in some years; usually not serious enough to threaten an established shrub.

propagation.season: Semi-ripe cuttings in summer root reasonably well in a gritty mix.
propagation.methods: Berries can also be sown fresh, though germination is often slow and erratic.
propagation.tips: Most gardeners buy young plants rather than propagating cotoneaster at home.
propagation.tracker: Propagation tracker coming soon — for now, add dates, method and result in your notes.

pollinator.value: Small flowers offer good simple nectar access for bees and hoverflies, and the heavy berry crop that follows is an important autumn and winter food source for birds.

timeline.Harvest: Berries Sep-Feb, gradually taken by birds
timeline.Pruning: Light trim after flowering, avoiding developing berries

gallery: Birds feeding on berries if captured

GK-COT-001.weeklyFact: Some cotoneaster species are considered invasive in certain regions because birds spread the berries so effectively — worth checking locally recommended varieties before planting new stock.
weeklyFacts: Some cotoneaster species are considered invasive in certain regions because birds spread the berries so effectively — worth checking locally recommended varieties before planting new stock.
A single well-berried cotoneaster can continue feeding garden birds well into late winter, often after most other berry sources have already been stripped.
GK-COT-001.pollinatorFact: Cotoneaster's small flowers are a good simple nectar source for bees and hoverflies in early summer, and its heavy, long-lasting berry crop is a genuinely important food source for birds through autumn and winter.

GK-DEU-001.name: Deutzia
GK-DEU-001.subtitle: Elegant, unfussy shrub smothered in small starry white flowers in late spring.

quickCare.Water: Water while establishing; drought-tolerant once settled
quickCare.Light: Full sun to part shade
quickCare.Feeding: Light spring feed if growth or flowering is poor
quickCare.Pruning: Prune immediately after flowering, removing some older stems at the base to renew the shrub

GK-DEU-001.notes: A genuinely low-fuss, reliable spring shrub — smothered in flower with very little input beyond a post-flowering tidy.
GK-DEU-001.medium: Peat-free compost with reasonable drainage for pots.

seasonalActions.Spring: Flower buds develop rapidly as the weather warms.
seasonalActions.Summer: Masses of small starry flowers open, then fade; prune immediately after.
seasonalActions.Autumn: New growth from the post-flowering prune matures and sets next year's buds.
seasonalActions.Winter: Deciduous and fully hardy; needs no winter protection.

problems.name: No flowers
problems.fix: Almost always pruned at the wrong time (winter, removing flower buds) — always prune right after flowering instead.

problems.name: Aphids on new growth
problems.fix: Wash off with water or encourage ladybirds; rarely serious.

propagation.season: Softwood cuttings in early summer root readily in a gritty mix.
propagation.methods: Semi-ripe cuttings in late summer are also a reliable alternative.
propagation.tips: A fairly easy, forgiving shrub to propagate from cuttings.
propagation.tracker: Propagation tracker coming soon — for now, add dates, method and result in your notes.

pollinator.value: Small, open starry flowers are easily accessed by bees, though the relatively brief flowering window limits its overall pollinator value compared to longer-blooming shrubs.

timeline.Pruning: Prune immediately after flowering, removing some old stems

GK-DEU-001.weeklyFact: Deutzia's small starry flowers are produced in such density that a mature shrub can appear almost entirely white for several weeks in late spring.
weeklyFacts: Deutzia's small starry flowers are produced in such density that a mature shrub can appear almost entirely white for several weeks in late spring.
The genus is named after Johan van der Deutz, an 18th-century Dutch patron who sponsored the botanical expeditions that first brought the plant to Europe.
GK-DEU-001.pollinatorFact: Deutzia's small open flowers are easily accessed by bees during its flowering window, though the relatively brief bloom period means it's best valued alongside longer-flowering companions.

GK-AME-001.name: Amelanchier
GK-AME-001.subtitle: Small multi-season tree — spring blossom, summer berries, and vivid autumn colour.

quickCare.Water: Water well for the first two years while establishing; drought-tolerant once settled
quickCare.Light: Full sun to part shade
quickCare.Feeding: No feeding usually needed once established
quickCare.Pruning: Minimal pruning needed; if required, do it while dormant in winter

GK-AME-001.notes: One of the best small trees for a limited space precisely because it earns its place three times a year: spring blossom, summer berries, and standout autumn colour.
GK-AME-001.soil: Any reasonable, moisture-retentive, well-drained soil, ideally slightly acidic.
GK-AME-001.medium: Not typically container-grown long-term; young trees can start in large pots before planting out.

seasonalActions.Spring: Delicate white blossom opens, often before leaves fully unfurl.
seasonalActions.Summer: Small dark berries ripen and are quickly taken by birds.
seasonalActions.Autumn: Leaves turn vivid orange-red — one of the best small-tree autumn colour displays available.
seasonalActions.Winter: Bare branch structure; fully hardy and undemanding.

problems.name: Fireblight
problems.fix: Can affect blossom and shoots; prune out and destroy affected wood promptly, disinfecting tools between cuts.

problems.name: Aphids on new growth
problems.fix: Usually minor; wash off with water or encourage ladybirds.

propagation.season: Semi-ripe cuttings in summer are possible but can be slow and inconsistent to root.
propagation.methods: Layering a low branch is a slower but more reliable amateur method.
propagation.tips: Most gardeners buy a young grafted or seed-grown tree rather than propagating at home.
propagation.tracker: Propagation tracker coming soon — for now, add dates, method and result in your notes.

pollinator.value: Spring blossom is a useful early-season nectar source for bees, and the summer berries that follow are quickly taken by birds, giving the tree wildlife value at two very different times of year.

timeline.Seeding: N/A — usually bought as young trees
timeline.Flowering: Apr, often before leaves fully unfurl
timeline.Harvest: Berries Jun-Jul, quickly taken by birds
timeline.Pruning: Minimal; if needed, prune while dormant in winter

GK-AME-001.weeklyFact: Amelanchier berries taste similar to a mild blueberry and are edible for people too, though birds usually strip a tree before there's much left to try.
weeklyFacts: Amelanchier berries taste similar to a mild blueberry and are edible for people too, though birds usually strip a tree before there's much left to try.
The common name 'snowy mespilus' refers to the tree's mass of white spring blossom, which can make a small tree look genuinely snow-covered for a week or two.
GK-AME-001.pollinatorFact: Amelanchier's spring blossom is a useful early-season nectar source for bees, and its summer berry crop is quickly taken by birds, giving the tree real wildlife value at two different points in the year.

GK-CRA-001.name: Crab Apple
GK-CRA-001.subtitle: Compact flowering tree with spring blossom and colourful autumn fruit for wildlife.

quickCare.Water: Water well for the first two years while establishing; drought-tolerant once settled
quickCare.Light: Full sun for the best blossom and fruit
quickCare.Feeding: No feeding usually needed once established in reasonable soil
quickCare.Pruning: Prune while dormant in winter if shaping is needed; naturally forms a good shape with little intervention

GK-CRA-001.notes: A genuinely useful garden tree beyond its own display — many crab apple varieties are excellent pollination partners for eating apples growing nearby, since they flower over a long, overlapping window.
GK-CRA-001.medium: Not typically container-grown long-term; young trees can start in large pots before planting out.

seasonalActions.Spring: Blossom opens in a mass of white or pink flowers, valuable for pollinating nearby apple trees too.
seasonalActions.Summer: Small fruit develops through summer.
seasonalActions.Autumn: Fruit ripens to red, orange or yellow depending on variety — some varieties hold fruit on the tree well into winter.
seasonalActions.Winter: Bare branch structure, sometimes still holding decorative fruit; fully hardy.

problems.name: Apple scab
problems.fix: Dark scabby patches on leaves and fruit; rake up and dispose of fallen leaves in autumn to reduce next year's spores.

problems.name: Fireblight
problems.fix: Can affect blossom and shoots; prune out and destroy affected wood promptly, disinfecting tools between cuts.

propagation.season: Grafting onto a specific rootstock is the standard method for named ornamental varieties.
propagation.methods: Species crab apple can be grown from seed, though named varieties won't come true this way.
propagation.tips: Most gardeners buy a grafted young tree rather than attempting to graft at home.
propagation.tracker: Propagation tracker coming soon — for now, add dates, method and result in your notes.

pollinator.value: Crab apple blossom is a major spring nectar and pollen source for bees, and many varieties are specifically valuable as pollination partners for eating apples flowering at the same time.

timeline.Seeding: N/A — usually bought as grafted young trees
timeline.Harvest: Fruit Sep-Dec, some varieties holding fruit into winter
timeline.Pruning: Prune while dormant in winter if needed

photoPlan.hero: Clear whole-plant photo of Crab Apple.
gallery: Birds feeding on fruit if captured

GK-CRA-001.weeklyFact: Many crab apple varieties are planted specifically as pollination partners for eating apple trees, since a lone eating apple often can't pollinate itself and needs a compatible flowering neighbour.
weeklyFacts: Many crab apple varieties are planted specifically as pollination partners for eating apple trees, since a lone eating apple often can't pollinate itself and needs a compatible flowering neighbour.
Some ornamental crab apple varieties hold their colourful fruit on the tree right through winter, providing a valuable late-season food source for birds when little else remains.
GK-CRA-001.pollinatorFact: Crab apple blossom is a major spring nectar and pollen source for bees, and it's frequently planted deliberately near eating apple trees as a reliable pollination partner.

GK-HOL-001.name: Holly
GK-HOL-001.subtitle: Classic spiky evergreen tree or hedge, with berries on female plants only.
tags: Needs male and female for berries

quickCare.Water: Water well while establishing; drought-tolerant once settled
quickCare.Light: Full sun to part shade
quickCare.Feeding: No feeding usually needed once established
quickCare.Pruning: Trim hedges or shape in late summer; avoid cutting during the main spring nesting season

GK-HOL-001.notes: Holly is dioecious — individual plants are either male or female, and only female plants produce berries, and only if a male plant is flowering somewhere reasonably nearby.
GK-HOL-001.medium: Peat-free compost with reasonable drainage for pots and topiary specimens.

seasonalActions.Spring: Small flowers open, easy to miss; both male and female flowers are needed nearby for berries to form.
seasonalActions.Summer: Watch for leaf miner damage, usually cosmetic rather than serious.
seasonalActions.Autumn: Berries ripen to red on female plants if a male is flowering nearby.
seasonalActions.Winter: Berries persist through winter, a valuable food source for birds in hard weather.

problems.name: Leaf miner
problems.fix: Pale winding trails in leaves; mostly cosmetic and rarely serious enough to need treatment.

problems.name: No berries
problems.fix: Either the plant is male, or there's no male holly flowering within pollinating range — berries need both sexes present.

propagation.season: Semi-ripe cuttings in late summer root slowly but reasonably reliably in a gritty mix.
propagation.methods: Named varieties are usually propagated from cuttings to guarantee sex and leaf variegation.
propagation.tips: Patience is essential — holly is naturally a slow grower from cuttings.
propagation.tracker: Propagation tracker coming soon — for now, add dates, method and result in your notes.

pollinator.value: Small spring flowers offer a modest but useful nectar source for early bees, and the berries that follow are an important winter food source for birds during hard weather.

timeline.Seeding: N/A — usually bought as young plants
timeline.Harvest: Berries Nov-Feb, valuable in hard winter weather
timeline.Pruning: Trim in late summer, avoiding spring nesting season

gallery: Whole tree or hedge showing form
Berries in winter on a female plant
Birds feeding on berries in hard weather if captured

GK-HOL-001.weeklyFact: Only female holly plants produce the classic red winter berries, and only if there's a male holly flowering somewhere within bee-pollinating range — a lone female without a nearby male will never berry.
weeklyFacts: Only female holly plants produce the classic red winter berries, and only if there's a male holly flowering somewhere within bee-pollinating range — a lone female without a nearby male will never berry.
Holly berries are toxic to people in quantity but are a critical winter food source for birds like blackbirds and thrushes, especially during hard frost when other food is scarce.
GK-HOL-001.pollinatorFact: Holly's small spring flowers are a modest but useful nectar source for early-season bees, and its winter berries — produced only on female plants pollinated by a nearby male — are a genuinely important food source for birds in hard weather.

GK-ESC-001.name: Escallonia
GK-ESC-001.subtitle: Glossy evergreen shrub with small pink or white flowers, tough enough for coastal hedging.

quickCare.Water: Water while establishing; drought-tolerant once settled
quickCare.Light: Full sun for the best flowering
quickCare.Feeding: Light spring feed if growth is poor
quickCare.Pruning: Trim after the main flowering flush; established hedges can be clipped again in late summer

GK-ESC-001.notes: Genuinely useful for exposed coastal gardens — few flowering evergreens tolerate salt-laden wind as well as escallonia.
GK-ESC-001.medium: Peat-free compost with reasonable drainage for pots.

seasonalActions.Spring: Trim lightly to shape once hard frost risk has passed.
seasonalActions.Summer: Main flush of small flowers opens; trim hedges after flowering.
seasonalActions.Autumn: A lighter second trim if needed to tidy hedging before winter.
seasonalActions.Winter: Evergreen; retains foliage through most winters in milder gardens.

problems.name: Leaf spot in humid weather
problems.fix: Remove affected leaves and improve airflow between plants.

problems.name: Frost damage in a hard winter
problems.fix: Choose a sheltered spot in colder inland gardens; coastal escallonia is more used to wind than deep frost.

propagation.season: Semi-ripe cuttings in summer root reasonably reliably in a gritty mix.
propagation.methods: A fairly easy shrub to propagate from cuttings with basic care.
propagation.tips: Hedging plants are often bought as young whips rather than propagated one at a time.
propagation.tracker: Propagation tracker coming soon — for now, add dates, method and result in your notes.

pollinator.value: Small tubular flowers are popular with bees, and a flowering escallonia hedge can be a genuinely busy pollinator spot in early summer.

timeline.Flowering: Jun-Jul, sometimes a lighter second flush
timeline.Pruning: Trim after flowering; hedges can take a second late-summer clip

gallery: Whole shrub or hedge in flower

GK-ESC-001.weeklyFact: Escallonia's glossy, slightly sticky leaves release a faint resinous scent when brushed against, a trait shared with several of its South American relatives.
weeklyFacts: Escallonia's glossy, slightly sticky leaves release a faint resinous scent when brushed against, a trait shared with several of its South American relatives.
Escallonia is a genuine coastal workhorse in the UK and Ireland, planted extensively along seafronts specifically for its wind and salt tolerance.
GK-ESC-001.pollinatorFact: Escallonia's small tubular flowers are popular with bees, and a flowering hedge in early summer can host a surprising amount of pollinator activity for a purely functional garden boundary.

GK-CHE-001.name: Flowering Cherry
GK-CHE-001.subtitle: Spectacular but brief spring blossom display on a classic garden and street tree.

quickCare.Water: Water well for the first two years while establishing; drought-tolerant once settled
quickCare.Light: Full sun for the best blossom display
quickCare.Feeding: No feeding usually needed once established
quickCare.Pruning: Prune only in summer if needed — winter or spring pruning risks silver leaf disease entering through cuts

GK-CHE-001.notes: Always prune stone-fruit trees like flowering cherry in summer, not winter — cutting during the dormant season significantly raises the risk of silver leaf disease entering through the wound.
GK-CHE-001.medium: Not typically container-grown long-term; young trees can start in large pots before planting out.

seasonalActions.Spring: Blossom opens in a dramatic, often very short-lived display, sometimes just one to two weeks.
seasonalActions.Summer: Full leaf canopy provides shade; a good time for any necessary pruning.
seasonalActions.Autumn: Leaves may show some autumn colour depending on variety.
seasonalActions.Winter: Bare branch structure; fully hardy and undemanding.

problems.name: Silver leaf disease
problems.fix: Enters through pruning wounds made in winter; always prune in summer instead, and remove any affected branches promptly.

problems.name: Bacterial canker
problems.fix: Causes sunken, oozing patches on bark; prune out affected wood in summer and avoid winter pruning, which increases infection risk.

propagation.season: Grafting onto a specific rootstock is the standard method for named ornamental varieties.
propagation.methods: Most gardeners buy a grafted young tree rather than attempting to graft flowering cherry at home.
propagation.tips: Species types can be grown from seed, though named varieties won't come true this way.
propagation.tracker: Propagation tracker coming soon — for now, add dates, method and result in your notes.

pollinator.value: Blossom is a valuable early-to-mid spring nectar source for bees, though the flowering window is often quite short compared to other blossom trees.

timeline.Seeding: N/A — usually bought as grafted young trees
timeline.Flowering: Mar-Apr, often a brief 1-2 week display
timeline.Pruning: Prune only in summer, never in winter or early spring

photoPlan.hero: Clear whole-plant photo of Flowering Cherry.
gallery: Fallen petals ('cherry blossom snow') if captured

GK-CHE-001.weeklyFact: Japan's centuries-old tradition of hanami (flower viewing) is built specifically around the fleeting nature of cherry blossom, celebrating a display that often lasts barely a week or two.
weeklyFacts: Japan's centuries-old tradition of hanami (flower viewing) is built specifically around the fleeting nature of cherry blossom, celebrating a display that often lasts barely a week or two.
Flowering cherry belongs to the genus Prunus, the same genus as edible plums, apricots and true cherries, though most ornamental varieties are bred purely for blossom rather than fruit.
GK-CHE-001.pollinatorFact: Flowering cherry's brief but abundant spring blossom is a valuable early-season nectar source for bees, concentrated into a short but intense flowering window.

GK-YEW-001.name: Yew
GK-YEW-001.subtitle: Ancient, extremely long-lived evergreen — a classic dense hedging and topiary conifer.

quickCare.Water: Water while establishing; drought-tolerant once settled
quickCare.Light: Full sun to full shade — one of very few conifers that tolerates deep shade well
quickCare.Feeding: No feeding usually needed once established
quickCare.Pruning: Clip hedges once or twice a year, typically late summer; tolerates hard renovation pruning better than almost any other conifer

GK-YEW-001.notes: Unusually tolerant of hard pruning for a conifer — an overgrown yew hedge can be cut back hard into old wood and will regenerate, which most conifers simply cannot do.
GK-YEW-001.soil: Any reasonable, well-drained soil — genuinely adaptable.
GK-YEW-001.medium: Peat-free compost with reasonable drainage for pots and topiary specimens.

seasonalActions.Spring: New soft growth flushes; avoid heavy pruning until it hardens slightly.
seasonalActions.Summer: Main hedge-clipping window, once growth has hardened.
seasonalActions.Autumn: A second, lighter trim if needed to tidy shape before winter.
seasonalActions.Winter: Fully hardy and evergreen; needs no protection.

problems.name: Vine weevil in containers
problems.fix: Notched leaf edges (adults) or collapsed plants (grubs eating roots); check compost and consider nematode treatment for pot-grown specimens.

problems.name: Waterlogging
problems.fix: Yew dislikes sitting in permanently wet soil far more than it dislikes drought; improve drainage in heavy, poorly drained ground.

propagation.season: Semi-ripe cuttings in autumn root slowly but reliably over many months.
propagation.methods: Named varieties are propagated from cuttings to guarantee habit and colour.
propagation.tips: Patience is essential — yew is naturally very slow-growing from cuttings, often the slowest shrub or tree in this whole library.
propagation.tracker: Propagation tracker coming soon — for now, add dates, method and result in your notes.

pollinator.value: Wind-pollinated with no petals or nectar; almost no direct pollinator value, though the red arils (berry-like fruit) that follow on female plants feed birds.

timeline.Seeding: N/A — usually bought as young plants
timeline.Flowering: Small inconspicuous flowers in spring, wind-pollinated
timeline.Harvest: Red arils on female plants Aug-Oct
timeline.Pruning: Clip hedges in late summer; tolerates hard renovation any time

gallery: Whole tree or hedge showing dense evergreen form
Close-up of foliage and red arils if present
Ancient/mature specimen for scale if available

GK-YEW-001.weeklyFact: Yew is one of the longest-lived trees in Europe — some churchyard yews are estimated to be well over a thousand years old, predating the churches built beside them.
weeklyFacts: Yew is one of the longest-lived trees in Europe — some churchyard yews are estimated to be well over a thousand years old, predating the churches built beside them.
Every part of a yew is highly toxic except the fleshy red aril surrounding the seed, which birds eat safely while the toxic seed itself usually passes through undigested.
GK-YEW-001.pollinatorFact: Yew is wind-pollinated and offers almost no direct nectar value to pollinators, but the red arils produced on female plants in autumn are an important food source for birds, and dense yew growth provides valuable year-round shelter.

GK-DAH2-001.name: Dahlia (from tuber)
GK-DAH2-001.subtitle: Same border favourite as the existing Dahlia entry, catalogued here specifically as a tuber for propagation tracking.

quickCare.Water: Water well once established, especially as buds form
quickCare.Light: Full sun
quickCare.Feeding: Weekly high-potash feed once flowering
quickCare.Pruning: Deadhead constantly; stake before top-heavy with flower

GK-DAH2-001.notes: If you're tracking this as a tuber going into a pot or seed tray rather than a mature plant already in the ground, this entry (rather than the main Dahlia one) is the better match.
GK-DAH2-001.soil: Fertile, well-drained soil enriched with organic matter.
GK-DAH2-001.medium: Peat-free compost with added grit for starting tubers under cover.

seasonalActions.Spring: Pot up tubers under cover from March; plant out after last frost.
seasonalActions.Summer: Water, feed and stake as growth continues.
seasonalActions.Autumn: Lift after first frost blackens foliage, or mulch heavily in mild areas.
seasonalActions.Winter: Store lifted tubers frost-free and just barely moist.

problems.name: Slugs on young shoots
problems.fix: Protect emerging growth with barriers or torchlight removal.

problems.name: Tuber rot in storage
problems.fix: Store somewhere cool, dark and just barely moist; discard any soft tubers before storing the rest.

propagation.season: Divide dormant tubers in spring, ensuring each division has a piece of old stem base with at least one eye.
propagation.methods: Basal cuttings from sprouted tubers in early spring multiply stock fastest.
propagation.tips: Label divisions carefully — dahlia tubers look near-identical once separated from the parent clump.
propagation.tracker: Propagation tracker coming soon — for now, add dates, method and result in your notes.

pollinator.value: Open-centred dahlias are far better for bees than very double forms.

timeline.Pruning: Lift tubers after first frost

photoPlan.hero: Clear whole-plant photo of Dahlia (from tuber).
gallery: Newly potted tuber with first shoots
Lifted tuber clump at end of season

GK-DAH2-001.weeklyFact: A dahlia tuber isn't a true bulb or root, but a swollen underground stem structure storing energy for the following year's growth.
weeklyFacts: A dahlia tuber isn't a true bulb or root, but a swollen underground stem structure storing energy for the following year's growth.
A single dahlia tuber clump can be divided into a dozen or more new plants over a few years if the divisions are done carefully.
GK-DAH2-001.pollinatorFact: Open, single-flowered dahlias are far more useful to bees than the fully double 'dinner-plate' forms, which can hide the pollen almost completely.

GK-FRI-001.name: Snake's Head Fritillary
GK-FRI-001.subtitle: Delicate, chequerboard-patterned bulb for damp meadows and border edges.

quickCare.Water: Keep moist, especially in spring — dislikes drying out during active growth
quickCare.Light: Full sun to part shade
quickCare.Feeding: No feeding needed in reasonable soil
quickCare.Pruning: No pruning; let foliage die back naturally after flowering

GK-FRI-001.notes: Genuinely unusual among bulbs for tolerating damp, even boggy ground — most bulbs rot in wet soil, but this one is naturally a damp meadow plant.
GK-FRI-001.soil: Moist, humus-rich soil; tolerates heavier, damper ground than most bulbs.
GK-FRI-001.medium: Peat-free compost kept consistently moist for containers.

seasonalActions.Spring: Distinctive chequered bell flowers open, nodding on thin stems.
seasonalActions.Summer: Foliage dies back after flowering — allow it to yellow naturally.
seasonalActions.Autumn: Bulbs are dormant; keep soil from drying out completely.
seasonalActions.Winter: Fully dormant; naturally suited to damp winter ground.

problems.name: Bulb rot in very dry, free-draining soil
problems.fix: Counter-intuitively, this bulb prefers damper ground than most — avoid overly sharp drainage.

problems.name: Slugs on emerging shoots
problems.fix: Protect with barriers or torchlight removal in early spring.

propagation.season: Bulbs produce small offsets that can be lifted and divided once foliage has died back.
propagation.methods: Self-seeds in favourable damp conditions, though seed-grown plants take several years to flower.
propagation.tips: Best left undisturbed once established, as it can be slow to recover from division.
propagation.tracker: Propagation tracker coming soon — for now, add dates, method and result in your notes.

pollinator.value: An early, distinctive nectar source for bees, with a nodding flower shape that suits certain bee species particularly well.

timeline.Pruning: No pruning; let foliage die back naturally

photoPlan.hero: Clear whole-plant photo of Snake's Head Fritillary.
gallery: Whole plant in a damp border or meadow setting

GK-FRI-001.weeklyFact: The chequerboard pattern on fritillary petals is a genuinely rare pigmentation pattern in the plant world, caused by a structural arrangement of pigment cells rather than a simple colour blend.
weeklyFacts: The chequerboard pattern on fritillary petals is a genuinely rare pigmentation pattern in the plant world, caused by a structural arrangement of pigment cells rather than a simple colour blend.
Snake's head fritillary is a UK native wildflower, historically found in damp hay meadows, though wild populations have declined significantly with changes in land use.
GK-FRI-001.pollinatorFact: Fritillary's nodding, bell-shaped flowers provide an early and somewhat unusual nectar source, well suited to bees able to approach and work the flower from below.

GK-CAM2-001.name: Camassia
GK-CAM2-001.subtitle: Tall spikes of vivid blue star-shaped flowers, excellent for naturalising in grass.

quickCare.Water: Water at planting; otherwise tolerates a range of moisture levels, including damper soil than many bulbs
quickCare.Light: Full sun to part shade
quickCare.Feeding: No feeding needed in reasonable soil
quickCare.Pruning: No pruning; if naturalising in grass, don't mow until foliage has died back

GK-CAM2-001.notes: An excellent, underused alternative to bluebells for naturalising in a damp lawn or meadow area, with the bonus of being later-flowering and extending the bulb season.
GK-CAM2-001.soil: Moist, humus-rich soil; tolerates heavier ground than most bulbs.
GK-CAM2-001.medium: Peat-free compost with added organic matter for containers.

seasonalActions.Spring: Tall flower spikes open in a vivid blue haze.
seasonalActions.Summer: Foliage dies back; safe to resume mowing over naturalised patches.
seasonalActions.Autumn: Plant new bulbs from September.
seasonalActions.Winter: Bulbs are fully dormant underground.

problems.name: Bulb rot in very free-draining, dry soil
problems.fix: Prefers damper ground than many bulbs; avoid overly sharp, dry conditions.

problems.name: Slow to establish
problems.fix: Give it a season or two to settle in before judging the display — camassia often improves markedly in its second year.

propagation.season: Bulbs produce small offsets that can be lifted and divided once foliage has died back.
propagation.methods: Self-seeds in favourable conditions, though seed-grown plants take several years to flower.
propagation.tips: Best left undisturbed for the first couple of years to establish well.
propagation.tracker: Propagation tracker coming soon — for now, add dates, method and result in your notes.

pollinator.value: An outstanding bee plant — tall, densely flowered spikes offer an unusually large amount of accessible nectar and pollen for a spring bulb.

timeline.Pruning: Don't mow naturalised patches until foliage dies back

gallery: Naturalised drift in grass or border

GK-CAM2-001.weeklyFact: Camassia bulbs were a genuine food source for some Indigenous peoples of the Pacific Northwest, traditionally roasted and eaten much like a sweet, mild onion.
weeklyFacts: Camassia bulbs were a genuine food source for some Indigenous peoples of the Pacific Northwest, traditionally roasted and eaten much like a sweet, mild onion.
Camassia flowers later than most spring bulbs, helping extend the naturalised bulb season by several weeks into early summer.
GK-CAM2-001.pollinatorFact: Camassia is an outstanding bee plant for its size — the tall, densely packed flower spikes offer an unusually large amount of accessible nectar and pollen for a spring-flowering bulb.

GK-NER-001.name: Nerine
GK-NER-001.subtitle: Vivid pink spider-like flowers that appear in autumn, often before or without leaves.

quickCare.Water: Water while in leaf through spring and summer; keep drier once dormant
quickCare.Light: Full sun, ideally against a warm, sheltered wall
quickCare.Feeding: Light feed while in leaf, spring to summer
quickCare.Pruning: No pruning; remove old dead foliage once it withers

GK-NER-001.notes: Plant with the bulb 'neck' at or just above soil level, and against a warm south-facing wall in cooler gardens — nerine flowers best when the bulb gets a proper summer baking.
GK-NER-001.soil: Free-draining soil; dislikes being disturbed once settled.
GK-NER-001.medium: Peat-free compost with added grit; a tight-fitting pot (nerines flower better when slightly pot-bound) if grown in containers.

seasonalActions.Spring: Foliage grows on from where it left off, or begins fresh after winter dormancy.
seasonalActions.Summer: Leaves continue growing, storing energy for the fantastic autumn display ahead.
seasonalActions.Autumn: Flower spikes emerge, often dramatically, sometimes with little to no leaf visible.
seasonalActions.Winter: Foliage may persist in mild gardens; leaves die back in colder ones.

problems.name: No flowers
problems.fix: Usually insufficient summer sun/heat to 'bake' the bulb, or the bulb was disturbed or planted too deep; ensure a warm, sheltered spot and shallow planting.

problems.name: Bulb rot in heavy, wet soil
problems.fix: Improve drainage significantly; nerine strongly dislikes sitting wet, especially in winter.

propagation.season: Bulbs produce small offsets that can be lifted and divided once dormant, though nerine dislikes disturbance and can sulk for a season after division.
propagation.methods: Best left undisturbed for several years to form a strong, floriferous clump.
propagation.tips: Most gardeners buy established bulbs rather than growing nerine from seed, which is very slow to reach flowering size.
propagation.tracker: Propagation tracker coming soon — for now, add dates, method and result in your notes.

pollinator.value: A valuable late-season nectar source for bees at a time of year when few other bulbs are in flower.

timeline.Seeding: Plant bulbs Jul-Aug, ideally undisturbed for years after
timeline.Pruning: Remove old foliage once it withers

gallery: Whole clump in flower, often with little foliage visible

GK-NER-001.weeklyFact: Nerine bowdenii is one of relatively few bulbs that flowers reliably in autumn, filling a genuine gap in the bulb calendar between summer and the first snowdrops of winter.
weeklyFacts: Nerine bowdenii is one of relatively few bulbs that flowers reliably in autumn, filling a genuine gap in the bulb calendar between summer and the first snowdrops of winter.
Nerine flowers so much better when left undisturbed and slightly pot-bound that deliberately restricting its roots is a genuine, recommended cultivation technique.
GK-NER-001.pollinatorFact: Nerine's autumn flowers are a valuable late-season nectar source for bees, flowering at a time when very few other bulbs are in bloom to compete for pollinator attention.

GK-ANE-001.name: Anemone
GK-ANE-001.subtitle: Low, daisy-like spring flowers that carpet ground under trees and shrubs.

quickCare.Water: Water at planting; otherwise relies on natural spring rainfall
quickCare.Light: Part shade, ideally under deciduous trees or shrubs
quickCare.Feeding: No feeding needed in reasonable soil
quickCare.Pruning: No pruning; let foliage die back naturally after flowering

GK-ANE-001.notes: Soak the small knobbly tubers overnight before planting — they're notoriously slow to take up water otherwise and can sit dormant for a frustratingly long time without this step.
GK-ANE-001.medium: Peat-free compost with added leaf mould for containers.

seasonalActions.Spring: Low, daisy-like flowers open in a carpet, closing at night and in dull weather.
seasonalActions.Summer: Foliage dies back after flowering.
seasonalActions.Autumn: Dormant underground beneath leaf litter.
seasonalActions.Winter: Fully dormant; the leaf litter above acts as natural winter protection.

problems.name: Slow or patchy establishment
problems.fix: Almost always from planting dry, unsoaked tubers; soak overnight before planting for much better results.

problems.name: Slugs on emerging shoots
problems.fix: Protect with barriers or torchlight removal in early spring.

propagation.season: Divide congested clumps once foliage has died back, though this species spreads well on its own once established.
propagation.methods: Self-seeds readily in favourable woodland conditions.
propagation.tips: Soaking tubers before planting is the single biggest factor in successful establishment, more so than propagation method.
propagation.tracker: Propagation tracker coming soon — for now, add dates, method and result in your notes.

pollinator.value: Open, daisy-like flowers give bees and other early-season insects easy access to pollen on mild spring days.

timeline.Seeding: Plant tubers Sep-Oct, soaked overnight first
timeline.Pruning: No pruning; let foliage die back naturally

gallery: Carpet of flowers under trees or shrubs

GK-ANE-001.weeklyFact: Anemone flowers close up at night and in overcast weather, only opening fully in direct sunlight — giving rise to the common name 'windflower' for how responsive they are to conditions.
weeklyFacts: Anemone flowers close up at night and in overcast weather, only opening fully in direct sunlight — giving rise to the common name 'windflower' for how responsive they are to conditions.
The knobbly tubers look completely dead and lifeless when dry, which is part of why soaking them before planting makes such a dramatic difference to how quickly they establish.
GK-ANE-001.pollinatorFact: Anemone's simple, open flowers give bees and other early-season pollinators easy access to pollen on mild spring days, when relatively few other woodland-floor flowers are available.

GK-KAL-002.name: Kalanchoe
GK-KAL-002.subtitle: Compact, long-flowering succulent houseplant with clusters of bright, tiny blooms.

quickCare.Water: Water thoroughly then let compost dry out fully between waterings
quickCare.Light: Bright light, some direct sun for the best flowering
quickCare.Feeding: Light feed during the growing season, reduced once flowering finishes
quickCare.Pruning: Deadhead spent flower clusters; trim leggy stems after flowering to keep it compact

GK-KAL-002.notes: Kalanchoe needs a period of long, uninterrupted darkness (naturally occurring in autumn/winter) to trigger flower buds — bright artificial light at night nearby can delay or prevent reflowering.
GK-KAL-002.medium: Free-draining cactus/succulent compost with added grit.

seasonalActions.Spring: Resume light feeding as growth and flowering pick up.
seasonalActions.Summer: Water more often but always allow full drying between waterings.
seasonalActions.Autumn: Reduce watering as growth slows; flower buds may begin forming as days shorten.
seasonalActions.Winter: Main flowering season for many plants, given the naturally longer nights.

problems.name: No reflowering
problems.fix: Usually not enough uninterrupted darkness at night; keep away from artificial light in the evening during autumn to encourage new buds.

problems.name: Root rot
problems.fix: From overwatering or poor drainage; repot into a grittier mix and reduce watering.

propagation.season: Stem cuttings root readily after the cut end has callused for a day or two.
propagation.methods: Leaf cuttings can also root, though less reliably than stem cuttings.
propagation.tips: One of the easier flowering succulents to propagate and share.
propagation.tracker: Propagation tracker coming soon — for now, add dates, method and result in your notes.

pollinator.value: Grown mainly as an ornamental flowering houseplant; occasional outdoor flowering in warm climates can attract some pollinators.

timeline.Flowering: Can flower for weeks at a time, often winter into spring
timeline.Pruning: Deadhead and trim after flowering to keep compact

GK-KAL-002.weeklyFact: Kalanchoe is a 'short-day' plant, meaning it sets flower buds in response to long nights rather than warm temperatures — commercial growers manipulate darkness, not heat, to schedule its flowering for sale.
weeklyFacts: Kalanchoe is a 'short-day' plant, meaning it sets flower buds in response to long nights rather than warm temperatures — commercial growers manipulate darkness, not heat, to schedule its flowering for sale.
Kalanchoe is named after Kalanchoe ceratophylla, a species described from a Chinese name for the genus recorded by 18th-century botanists.
GK-KAL-002.pollinatorFact: Kalanchoe is grown mainly indoors and has limited typical pollinator access; where grown outdoors in warm climates its small clustered flowers can attract some visiting insects.

GK-LIT-001.name: Living Stones
GK-LIT-001.subtitle: Extraordinary succulents that mimic pebbles, needing very careful, infrequent watering.

quickCare.Water: Water very sparingly and only at specific times of year — overwatering is by far the most common way to lose this plant
quickCare.Light: Full sun, as much bright light as possible
quickCare.Feeding: No feeding needed; a genuinely undemanding plant once its watering rhythm is understood
quickCare.Pruning: No pruning needed; the plant naturally sheds and replaces its 'leaves' each year

GK-LIT-001.notes: The single most important thing to know: do NOT water during the plant's dormant resting phase or while it's splitting into new leaves, even if it looks shrivelled — this is normal and watering at the wrong time is the most common cause of death.
GK-LIT-001.medium: Very free-draining mineral/cactus mix with minimal organic content and plenty of grit or sand.

seasonalActions.Spring: Old leaf pair shrivels as a new pair emerges from within — do not water heavily during this transition.
seasonalActions.Summer: Water very lightly, only once the new leaf pair is fully formed and the old one has fully dried up.
seasonalActions.Autumn: A more typical light watering period, still allowing full drying between.
seasonalActions.Winter: Fully dormant and resting; do not water at all during this period, regardless of appearance.

problems.name: Splitting/bursting from overwatering during dormancy
problems.fix: The most common lithops death; research the specific watering calendar for this genus rather than treating it like a typical succulent.

problems.name: Stretching, losing its pebble-like compact shape
problems.fix: Insufficient light; move to the brightest possible spot, ideally with direct sun.

propagation.season: Seed is the traditional method but is slow, taking years to reach a mature size.
propagation.methods: Established clumps can occasionally be divided at natural separation points.
propagation.tips: Most owners buy an established plant rather than attempting propagation, given how slow and specialised it is.
propagation.tracker: Propagation tracker coming soon — for now, add dates, method and result in your notes.

pollinator.value: Flowers open in autumn and can attract some pollinators outdoors in their native South African habitat, though this is rarely relevant for an indoor-grown plant.

timeline.Flowering: Autumn, a single daisy-like flower per head
timeline.Pruning: None; leaves are naturally shed and replaced annually

GK-LIT-001.weeklyFact: Lithops evolved their pebble-like appearance specifically to avoid being eaten by animals in their native semi-desert habitat, blending in almost perfectly with surrounding stones.
weeklyFacts: Lithops evolved their pebble-like appearance specifically to avoid being eaten by animals in their native semi-desert habitat, blending in almost perfectly with surrounding stones.
Each lithops plant only ever has one pair of leaves at a time — the old pair is fully absorbed and replaced by a new pair from within, once a year.
GK-LIT-001.pollinatorFact: Lithops flowers, when they appear in autumn, can attract pollinators in their native South African habitat, though for most indoor-grown plants this is rarely observed.

GK-STA-001.name: String of Pearls
GK-STA-001.subtitle: Distinctive trailing succulent with round, bead-like leaves, striking in a hanging pot.

quickCare.Water: Water thoroughly then let compost dry out fully between waterings; the round leaves store water, so err on the side of underwatering
quickCare.Light: Bright indirect light, some direct sun for the healthiest growth
quickCare.Feeding: Light feed once or twice in the growing season
quickCare.Pruning: Trim trailing stems to control length; remove any shrivelled or dead sections

GK-STA-001.notes: The individual round 'pearls' are genuinely water-storage organs, similar in function to the paddles on other succulents just in a very different shape — treat watering the same as any drought-tolerant succulent.
GK-STA-001.medium: Free-draining cactus/succulent compost with added grit.

seasonalActions.Spring: Resume light watering as light levels increase.
seasonalActions.Summer: Water more often but always allow full drying between waterings.
seasonalActions.Autumn: Reduce watering as growth slows.
seasonalActions.Winter: Water very sparingly — once a month or less.

problems.name: Shrivelled, deflated 'pearls'
problems.fix: Sign of underwatering, though this species tolerates it well; water thoroughly and it usually plumps back up.

problems.name: Mushy, translucent pearls
problems.fix: Overwatering; reduce frequency significantly and check the pot has good drainage.

propagation.season: Stem cuttings root very easily — lay a trimmed strand across gritty compost and it will root along its length within a few weeks.
propagation.methods: One of the easiest succulents in this library to propagate.
propagation.tips: A popular cutting to share, since a single healthy plant can produce many trimmed lengths.
propagation.tracker: Propagation tracker coming soon — for now, add dates, method and result in your notes.

pollinator.value: Grown almost entirely indoors as an ornamental trailing succulent, with minimal typical pollinator relevance.

timeline.Flowering: Occasional small white, faintly cinnamon-scented flowers
timeline.Pruning: Trim trailing stems to control length as needed

photoPlan.hero: Clear whole-plant photo of String of Pearls.
gallery: Whole trailing plant in a hanging pot
Close-up of the round pearl-like leaves

GK-STA-001.weeklyFact: The round, pea-like leaves of string of pearls are a genuine water-storage adaptation, evolved to minimise surface area exposed to hot, dry air in its native South African habitat.
weeklyFacts: The round, pea-like leaves of string of pearls are a genuine water-storage adaptation, evolved to minimise surface area exposed to hot, dry air in its native South African habitat.
A trailing strand of string of pearls can grow to well over a metre long in good conditions, cascading dramatically from a hanging pot or high shelf.
GK-STA-001.pollinatorFact: String of pearls is grown almost entirely indoors and has limited typical pollinator access; its occasional small flowers have a faint cinnamon scent but are rarely a significant nectar source in a home setting.

GK-BUR-001.name: Burro's Tail
GK-BUR-001.subtitle: Dramatic trailing succulent with tightly packed, plump blue-green leaves.

quickCare.Water: Water thoroughly then let compost dry out fully between waterings
quickCare.Light: Bright light, some direct sun for the best colour and compact growth
quickCare.Feeding: Light feed once or twice in the growing season
quickCare.Pruning: Avoid handling the trailing stems more than necessary; trim only where genuinely needed

GK-BUR-001.notes: The leaves detach extremely easily at the slightest touch — position it somewhere it won't be brushed against often, and avoid moving or repotting it more than necessary.
GK-BUR-001.medium: Free-draining cactus/succulent compost with added grit.

seasonalActions.Spring: Resume light watering as light levels increase.
seasonalActions.Summer: Water more often but always allow full drying between waterings.
seasonalActions.Autumn: Reduce watering as growth slows.
seasonalActions.Winter: Water very sparingly — once a month or less.

problems.name: Leaves dropping at the slightest touch
problems.fix: Completely normal for this species; handle as little as possible and choose a stable, undisturbed spot.

problems.name: Shrivelled leaves
problems.fix: Sign of underwatering; water thoroughly, though this species tolerates dry spells better than overwatering.

propagation.season: Fallen or gently removed leaves will often root on their own if simply left on top of gritty compost.
propagation.methods: Stem cuttings also root readily, handled as little as possible.
propagation.tips: One of the easiest succulents to propagate almost by accident, given how easily leaves detach and root where they fall.
propagation.tracker: Propagation tracker coming soon — for now, add dates, method and result in your notes.

pollinator.value: Grown almost entirely indoors as an ornamental trailing succulent, with minimal typical pollinator relevance.

timeline.Flowering: Occasional small pink-red star-shaped flowers on mature plants
timeline.Pruning: Avoid unnecessary handling; trim only where needed

GK-BUR-001.weeklyFact: Burro's tail leaves are covered in a fine powdery coating called farina, which helps reflect intense sun and reduce water loss in its native hot, dry habitat — try not to rub it off when handling.
weeklyFacts: Burro's tail leaves are covered in a fine powdery coating called farina, which helps reflect intense sun and reduce water loss in its native hot, dry habitat — try not to rub it off when handling.
A well-grown, undisturbed burro's tail can take several years to develop its full dramatic trailing length, since growth is naturally slow and handling damage resets progress.
GK-BUR-001.pollinatorFact: Burro's tail is grown almost entirely indoors and has limited typical pollinator access; its occasional small star-shaped flowers are a minor feature rather than a significant nectar source.

GK-CAL2-001.name: Calathea
GK-CAL2-001.subtitle: Striking patterned foliage houseplant that folds its leaves up at night.

quickCare.Water: Keep consistently moist, ideally with rainwater or filtered water — sensitive to tap water minerals and chlorine
quickCare.Light: Bright indirect light; direct sun fades and scorches the patterned leaves
quickCare.Feeding: Light feed in the growing season, reduced in winter
quickCare.Pruning: Remove any damaged or fully browned leaves at the base

GK-CAL2-001.notes: Genuinely humidity-loving — a dry centrally-heated room is the most common reason this plant struggles, and a pebble tray or humidifier makes a real difference.
GK-CAL2-001.medium: Peat-free houseplant compost with added bark and perlite for an airy, moisture-retentive mix.

seasonalActions.Spring: Resume regular feeding as growth picks up.
seasonalActions.Summer: Water consistently and maintain humidity through the warmest, driest part of the year.
seasonalActions.Autumn: Reduce feeding as growth slows.
seasonalActions.Winter: Water slightly less; keep away from cold draughts and radiators.

problems.name: Crispy brown leaf edges
problems.fix: Usually low humidity or tap water minerals; increase humidity and switch to rainwater or filtered water if possible.

problems.name: Curling leaves
problems.fix: Often underwatering or low humidity combined; check both before assuming just one cause.

propagation.season: Division of an established clump at repotting time is the most reliable method.
propagation.methods: Each division needs a healthy piece of root system attached.
propagation.tips: Not typically grown from cuttings, unlike many trailing houseplants.
propagation.tracker: Propagation tracker coming soon — for now, add dates, method and result in your notes.

pollinator.value: Grown entirely indoors and has no access to outdoor pollinators; its rarely-seen flowers are a curiosity rather than a nectar source.

timeline.Pruning: Remove damaged leaves at the base as needed

gallery: Leaves folded up at night if captured

GK-CAL2-001.weeklyFact: Calathea leaves visibly rise and fold together at night and lower again during the day, a movement called nyctinasty that's thought to help the plant shed rainwater and track available light in its native rainforest floor habitat.
weeklyFacts: Calathea leaves visibly rise and fold together at night and lower again during the day, a movement called nyctinasty that's thought to help the plant shed rainwater and track available light in its native rainforest floor habitat.
Calathea is closely related to prayer plants (Maranta), sharing the same nightly leaf-folding movement and similarly precise humidity preferences.
GK-CAL2-001.pollinatorFact: Calathea is grown entirely indoors and has no access to outdoor pollinators; its value is purely ornamental, prized for its patterned foliage and distinctive daily leaf movement.

GK-PHIL-001.name: Philodendron
GK-PHIL-001.subtitle: Easy, trailing, heart-leaved houseplant, a forgiving classic for beginners.

quickCare.Water: Water when the top of the compost is dry; tolerates occasional missed waterings well
quickCare.Light: Tolerates low light but grows faster and fuller in bright indirect light
quickCare.Feeding: Light monthly feed in the growing season
quickCare.Pruning: Trim trailing stems to control length; pinch tips to encourage bushier growth

GK-PHIL-001.notes: Very easily confused with pothos in shops — philodendron leaves have a smooth, unmarked heart shape and a slightly softer texture, while pothos leaves are usually thicker and often marbled.
GK-PHIL-001.medium: Peat-free houseplant compost with reasonable drainage.

seasonalActions.Spring: Resume feeding as growth picks up.
seasonalActions.Summer: Water slightly more often; trim trailing stems as needed.
seasonalActions.Autumn: Reduce feeding.
seasonalActions.Winter: Water sparingly; growth slows noticeably indoors over winter.

problems.name: Yellowing lower leaves
problems.fix: Most often overwatering; let the compost dry out more between waterings.

problems.name: Leggy growth with few leaves
problems.fix: Usually too little light; move closer to a bright window and trim to encourage bushier regrowth.

propagation.season: Stem cuttings with a node root very easily in a glass of water within a couple of weeks.
propagation.methods: Once rooted, pot up several cuttings together for a fuller-looking plant.
propagation.tips: One of the easiest and most reliable houseplants to propagate and share.
propagation.tracker: Propagation tracker coming soon — for now, add dates, method and result in your notes.

pollinator.value: Grown indoors and has no access to outdoor pollinators — value here is purely ornamental.

timeline.Pruning: Trim trailing stems as needed to control shape

GK-PHIL-001.weeklyFact: Philodendron is one of the most tolerant common houseplants for low light, genuinely able to survive (though not thrive) in spots too dim for most other foliage plants.
weeklyFacts: Philodendron is one of the most tolerant common houseplants for low light, genuinely able to survive (though not thrive) in spots too dim for most other foliage plants.
The name Philodendron comes from Greek for tree-loving, describing how many species climb trees in their native rainforest habitat using aerial roots.
GK-PHIL-001.pollinatorFact: Philodendron is grown indoors and has no access to outdoor pollinators; its value is purely ornamental, and it's a popular beginner plant thanks to its tolerance of low light and irregular care.

GK-DRA-001.name: Dracaena
GK-DRA-001.subtitle: Architectural, upright houseplant with strappy leaves, tolerant of considerable neglect.

quickCare.Water: Water when the top few centimetres of compost are dry; sensitive to fluoride in tap water, which can cause brown leaf tips
quickCare.Light: Tolerates low light but grows better in bright indirect light
quickCare.Feeding: Light feed in the growing season only
quickCare.Pruning: Remove any yellowing or browned lower leaves at the base

GK-DRA-001.notes: Sensitive to fluoride and chlorine in tap water, which shows up as brown leaf tips — switching to rainwater or letting tap water stand overnight before use often clears this up.
GK-DRA-001.medium: Peat-free houseplant compost with reasonable drainage.

seasonalActions.Spring: Resume light feeding as growth picks up.
seasonalActions.Summer: Water slightly more often in warm conditions.
seasonalActions.Autumn: Reduce feeding.
seasonalActions.Winter: Water sparingly; growth slows noticeably indoors over winter.

problems.name: Brown leaf tips
problems.fix: Usually tap water minerals or fluoride sensitivity; switch to rainwater or filtered water.

problems.name: Toxic to cats and dogs if chewed
problems.fix: Keep out of reach of pets that like to chew houseplant leaves, or choose a pet-safe alternative if this is a concern.

propagation.season: Stem cuttings ('canes') root reasonably well in water or moist compost.
propagation.methods: Air layering is possible on a mature cane for a larger specimen.
propagation.tips: A moderately easy houseplant to propagate, though slower than trailing varieties like pothos.
propagation.tracker: Propagation tracker coming soon — for now, add dates, method and result in your notes.

pollinator.value: Grown indoors and has no access to outdoor pollinators — value here is purely ornamental.

timeline.Flowering: Occasionally flowers indoors with a strong, sweet night-time scent, though uncommon
timeline.Pruning: Remove yellowing lower leaves as needed

gallery: Whole plant showing upright architectural form
Flower spike if it appears, rare indoors

GK-DRA-001.weeklyFact: Despite the common name 'corn plant,' Dracaena fragrans is unrelated to actual corn — the name comes from its strappy, corn-leaf-like foliage rather than any botanical relationship.
weeklyFacts: Despite the common name 'corn plant,' Dracaena fragrans is unrelated to actual corn — the name comes from its strappy, corn-leaf-like foliage rather than any botanical relationship.
Dracaena fragrans is sometimes called mass cane in the houseplant trade, since young plants are often sold as several cut canes potted together.
GK-DRA-001.pollinatorFact: Dracaena is grown indoors and has no access to outdoor pollinators; on the rare occasion it flowers indoors, the intensely sweet night-time fragrance hints at its natural moth-pollination strategy in the wild.

GK-BEG-001.name: Begonia
GK-BEG-001.subtitle: Dramatically patterned foliage houseplant, grown for leaves rather than flowers.

quickCare.Water: Keep lightly moist; avoid getting water on the leaves, which can cause spotting
quickCare.Light: Bright indirect light; direct sun scorches the patterned leaves
quickCare.Feeding: Light feed in the growing season
quickCare.Pruning: Remove any damaged or fully faded leaves at the base

GK-BEG-001.notes: Grown almost entirely for its extraordinary leaf patterns and colours rather than its small, relatively insignificant flowers — don't be disappointed if it rarely blooms indoors.
GK-BEG-001.medium: Peat-free houseplant compost with added perlite for good drainage and airflow around the roots.

seasonalActions.Spring: Resume light feeding as growth picks up.
seasonalActions.Summer: Water consistently but avoid wetting the leaves; increase humidity in hot, dry spells.
seasonalActions.Autumn: Reduce feeding as growth slows.
seasonalActions.Winter: Water more sparingly; keep away from cold draughts.

problems.name: Powdery mildew
problems.fix: More likely in still, humid air with poor airflow; space away from other plants and avoid wetting leaves when watering.

problems.name: Leaf spotting
problems.fix: Usually from water sitting on the leaves; water at the base of the plant instead.

propagation.season: Leaf cuttings are the classic propagation method — a leaf laid flat with cuts across major veins can produce multiple new plantlets.
propagation.methods: Rhizome division is also possible for rhizomatous types.
propagation.tips: A genuinely fun, rewarding propagation project once you've seen how reliably a single leaf can multiply.

timeline.Flowering: Small flowers occasionally appear but are a minor feature

GK-BEG-001.weeklyFact: Rex begonia leaves can be propagated from a single leaf laid flat on moist compost with cuts made across the main veins — each cut can sprout an entirely new baby plant, a genuinely striking thing to watch happen.

There are over 2,000 recognised begonia species, ranging from tiny groundcover types to large shrub-like forms, though rex begonias are grown almost entirely for their leaves.
GK-BEG-001.pollinatorFact: Rex begonia is grown almost entirely indoors for its foliage; its infrequent, small flowers offer limited pollinator relevance in a typical home setting.

GK-CRAS-001.name: String of Hearts
GK-CRAS-001.subtitle: Delicate trailing succulent vine with heart-shaped, marbled leaves on thread-thin stems.

quickCare.Water: Water thoroughly then let compost dry out fully between waterings; grows from a small tuber that stores water
quickCare.Light: Bright indirect light, some direct sun for the best leaf colour
quickCare.Feeding: Light feed once or twice in the growing season
quickCare.Pruning: Trim trailing stems if extremely long; small aerial tubers can be left in place or removed and potted

GK-CRAS-001.notes: Often produces small bead-like aerial tubers along its trailing stems — these are a genuine, easy propagation shortcut, not a sign of a problem.
GK-CRAS-001.medium: Free-draining cactus/succulent compost with added grit.

seasonalActions.Spring: Resume light watering as light levels increase.
seasonalActions.Summer: Water more often but always allow full drying between waterings.
seasonalActions.Autumn: Reduce watering as growth slows.
seasonalActions.Winter: Water very sparingly — once a month or less.

problems.name: Leaf drop
problems.fix: Usually overwatering or a sudden change in conditions; reduce watering frequency and keep conditions stable.

problems.name: Faded leaf colour
problems.fix: Often too little light; move to a brighter spot with some direct sun for the best marbled colour.

propagation.season: Small aerial tubers that form along the stems can be potted directly, often already partly rooted.
propagation.methods: Stem cuttings with a node also root readily in water or moist compost.
propagation.tips: One of the easiest and most novel houseplants to propagate, thanks to the self-forming tubers.
propagation.tracker: Propagation tracker coming soon — for now, add dates, method and result in your notes.

pollinator.value: Grown almost entirely indoors as an ornamental trailing succulent, with minimal typical pollinator relevance.

timeline.Pruning: Trim trailing stems if very long

GK-CRAS-001.weeklyFact: String of hearts grows from an underground tuber and also forms small aerial tubers along its stems — a genuine backup water-and-energy store that makes it remarkably drought-tolerant for such a delicate-looking plant.
weeklyFacts: String of hearts grows from an underground tuber and also forms small aerial tubers along its stems — a genuine backup water-and-energy store that makes it remarkably drought-tolerant for such a delicate-looking plant.

Its unusual tubular flowers are specifically shaped to temporarily trap small flies, which pick up pollen before the flower releases them to visit another bloom — a pollination strategy called a 'flower trap.'
GK-CRAS-001.pollinatorFact: String of hearts is grown almost entirely indoors and has limited typical pollinator access; its unusual tubular flowers use a temporary insect-trapping strategy to achieve pollination in their native habitat.

GK-FIC-001.name: Fiddle Leaf Fig
GK-FIC-001.subtitle: Statement houseplant with huge glossy violin-shaped leaves — striking but genuinely fussy.

quickCare.Water: Water when the top few centimetres of compost are dry; consistency matters more than quantity for this species
quickCare.Light: Bright indirect light; some gentle direct morning sun is fine, but avoid harsh afternoon sun and low light alike
quickCare.Feeding: Light feed in the growing season
quickCare.Pruning: Wipe dust off the large leaves regularly; remove any damaged leaves at the base

GK-FIC-001.notes: Genuinely dislikes change — a fiddle leaf fig moved to a new spot, repotted, or exposed to a sudden temperature swing will often drop leaves in protest before settling down again over the following weeks.
GK-FIC-001.medium: Peat-free houseplant compost with added bark and perlite for good drainage and airflow.

seasonalActions.Spring: Resume regular feeding as growth picks up.
seasonalActions.Summer: Water consistently; this is the main growing season for new leaves.
seasonalActions.Autumn: Reduce feeding as growth slows.
seasonalActions.Winter: Water more sparingly and keep well away from cold draughts and heating vents.

problems.name: Brown spots on leaves
problems.fix: Usually overwatering or inconsistent watering; let the top of the compost dry out properly between waterings.

problems.name: Leaf drop after moving or repotting
problems.fix: Normal transplant/relocation stress; keep conditions otherwise stable and it typically recovers over several weeks.

propagation.season: Stem or single-leaf cuttings with a node can root in water, though it's slower and less reliable than many other houseplants.
propagation.methods: Air layering on a mature stem is a more reliable method for a larger specimen.
propagation.tips: Most owners buy an established young plant rather than propagating fiddle leaf fig at home, given how fussy it can be to root.
propagation.tracker: Propagation tracker coming soon — for now, add dates, method and result in your notes.

pollinator.value: Grown indoors and has no access to outdoor pollinators — value here is purely ornamental.

timeline.Pruning: Wipe leaves regularly; remove damaged leaves as needed

weeklyFacts: In the wild, Ficus lyrata can grow into a substantial forest tree over 12 metres tall, a considerable contrast to the single-stemmed potted specimens usually seen indoors.
Fiddle leaf fig became one of the most fashionable houseplants of the 2010s, largely thanks to its dramatic sculptural leaves photographing so well in interior design.
GK-FIC-001.pollinatorFact: Fiddle leaf fig is grown indoors and has no access to outdoor pollinators; its value is purely ornamental, prized for its dramatic large leaves despite a reputation for being fussy about change.
